Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Discharge Summary for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Bengali (code=bn, script=Bengali). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=West Bengal, district=Birbhum.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Bengali for Bengali.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 60
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Urban
- state: West Bengal
- district: Birbhum
- first_language: Bengali
- clinical_persona_summary: A 60-year-old male, currently married, retired, and functionally illiterate, resides in an urban area of West Bengal. His primary language is Bengali, and he exhibits a structured yet anxious approach to daily life, often managing cash savings and oral storytelling. He has a history of community festival planning and a preference for local cultural activities, with limited engagement in long-distance travel.

Document format guide for Discharge Summary (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Discharge Summary — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] DOB [[DOB|1994-06-21]] [[AGE|30]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|...]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]] | Course / advice: <concise paragraphs matching domain + persona sex/age>

MANDATORY inline entities (14 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ADMISSION_NUMBER — Admission Number e.g. [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- ABHA_ADDRESS — ABHA Address e.g. [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
- CASTE — Caste e.g. [[CASTE|OBC]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text:
ডিসচার্জ সামারি — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Birbhum District Hospital]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Subrata Chatterjee]] জন্ম তারিখ [[DOB|1964-08-15]] [[AGE|60]] [[GENDER|Male]]
ভর্তি [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-1128]] ওয়ার্ড [[WARD_NUMBER|A3]] বেড [[BED_NUMBER|07]]
ডাক্তার [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Amitava Sen]] | কোর্স / পরামর্শ: রোগী একজন 60 বছর বয়সী পুরুষ, অবসরপ্রাপ্ত, ক্রনিক অবস্ট্রাক্টিভ পালমোনারি ডিজিজ (COPD) এবং কমিউনিটি-অ্যাকোয়ার্ড নিউমোনিয়ার তীব্র বৃদ্ধির কারণে ভর্তি হয়েছিলেন। তাকে ইন্ট্রাভেনাস অ্যান্টিবায়োটিক, ব্রঙ্কোডাইলেটর এবং সিস্টেমিক কর্টিকোস্টেরয়েড দিয়ে চিকিৎসা করা হয়েছিল। SpO2 >92% বজায় রাখার জন্য নাসাল ক্যানুলার মাধ্যমে অক্সিজেন থেরাপি শুরু করা হয়েছিল। ভর্তির তৃতীয় দিনে, রোগীর উল্লেখযোগ্য ক্লিনিকাল উন্নতি দেখা গেছে, যার মধ্যে শ্বাসকষ্ট হ্রাস, উৎপাদনশীল কাশি কমে যাওয়া এবং স্থিতিশীল ভাইটালস অন্তর্ভুক্ত। পরবর্তীতে তাকে সাপ্লিমেন্টাল অক্সিজেন থেকে সরিয়ে নেওয়া হয় এবং ওরাল অ্যান্টিবায়োটিক দেওয়া শুরু হয়। রোগী এবং তার স্ত্রীকে ইনহেলার কৌশল, ধূমপান ত্যাগের গুরুত্ব এবং নিয়মিত ফলো-আপের প্রয়োজনীয়তা সম্পর্কে শিক্ষিত করা হয়েছে। তাকে ডিসচার্জের দুই সপ্তাহ পরে পোস্ট-ডিসচার্জ পর্যালোচনার জন্য ও.পি.ডি-তে যাওয়ার পরামর্শ দেওয়া হয়েছে।

[[RELATIVE_NAME|Anita Chatterjee]]
[[DISTRICT|Birbhum]]
[[PHONE_NUMBER|9832145678]]
[[ABHA_ADDRESS|subrata.c@abdm]]
[[CASTE|SC]]

persona: Pradip Mandal is a 60‑year‑old community caretaker who blends structured festival planning with a laid‑back, sometimes anxious, approach to life, and they often juggle cash savings, oral storytelling, and a love for Bengali folk culture.
cultural_background: Pradip Mandal, a 60‑year‑old Hindu from Birbhum, West Bengal, embodies the Bengali cultural milieu typical of the region. He grew up in a joint family, celebrates Durga Puja, Saraswati Puja, and other Hindu festivals with local customs. The urban setting of Birbhum has introduced him to a blend of traditional rural values and modern city life, yet he remains rooted in the Bengali language, folk music, and cuisine. His cultural identity is shaped by the agrarian heritage of Birbhum, the emphasis on community gatherings, and the influence of Bengali literature and cinema, even though he is illiterate.
linguistic_background: Pradip's mother tongue is Bengali, which he speaks fluently and uses for everyday communication in Birbhum. He has never learned to read or write Bengali or any other script, so he is functionally illiterate. He understands some spoken Hindi from television and radio, but does not use it actively. He has no proficiency in English. His linguistic abilities are therefore limited to oral Bengali, with very limited receptive Hindi and no literacy.
skills_and_expertise: Pradip is adept at preparing traditional Bengali dishes such as shorshe ilish, roshogolla, and luchi, and he manages household chores efficiently despite his illiteracy. He has practical experience in gardening and maintaining a small vegetable patch, basic carpentry for household repairs, and arranging local festivals and puja events. His expertise includes budgeting using cash, caring for his family members, especially grandchildren, and narrating Bengali folk stories and songs. He also has a good understanding of community customs and can coordinate collective activities in his neighbourhood.

hobbies_and_interests: Pradip enjoys listening to Rabindra Sangeet and other Bengali folk songs, watching classic Bengali films on TV, and spending time in his vegetable garden. He likes cooking traditional dishes for family gatherings and recounting folk stories to his grandchildren. He finds relaxation in short walks around his neighborhood, attending temple puja, and playing cards with friends at the community center. Occasionally, he follows news on the radio to stay connected with the world.

career_goals_and_ambitions: Pradip aspires to maintain his health and emotional well‑being, seeking ways to manage his anxiety through meditation and community support. He wishes to learn basic reading and writing in Bengali to be able to read religious texts and pass on stories to his grandchildren. Additionally, Pradip aims to provide financial stability for his family by continuing his small cash‑savings activities and advising younger relatives on prudent money management.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

Document format guide for Referral Letter (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.

কারণ: রোগীর 6 মাস ধরে ডান দিকের কুঁচকিতে ব্যথাযুক্ত এবং না কমানো যায় এমন ইনগুইনাল হার্নিয়া রয়েছে, যার জন্য অস্ত্রোপচারের প্রয়োজন।

রোগী 60 বছর বয়সী একজন অবসরপ্রাপ্ত পুরুষ, যিনি বীরভূম, পশ্চিমবঙ্গ থেকে এসেছেন এবং বর্তমানে বিবাহিত। তিনি একজন বাংলাভাষী এবং তাঁর পড়াশোনা করার ক্ষমতা সীমিত। রোগী জানিয়েছেন যে গত ছয় মাস ধরে তাঁর ডান দিকের কুঁচকির ফোলাভাব ধীরে ধীরে বাড়ছে। তিনি দাঁড়ালে বা চাপ দিলে এই ফোলাভাব আরও স্পষ্ট হয় এবং গত এক মাস ধরে এটি ক্রমশ বেদনাদায়ক হয়ে উঠেছে। তিনি এই অংশে কোনো আঘাতের ইতিহাস অস্বীকার করেছেন। তাঁর জ্বর, কাঁপুনি বা মলত্যাগের অভ্যাসে কোনো পরিবর্তন নেই। তিনি মাঝে মাঝে কোষ্ঠকাঠিন্যের কথা জানিয়েছেন।

পরীক্ষায় দেখা গেছে, ডান দিকের ইনগুইনাল অঞ্চলে 6x4 সেমি আকারের একটি বেদনাদায়ক এবং না কমানো যায় এমন পিণ্ড অনুভূত হচ্ছে, যা অণ্ডকোষের দিকে বিস্তৃত। এর উপরের ত্বক স্বাভাবিক রঙের এবং তাপমাত্রার। অন্ত্রে বাধার কোনো লক্ষণ নেই। পেটের বাকি পরীক্ষা স্বাভাবিক।

পরিকল্পনা: রোগীকে মূল্যায়নের জন্য এবং সম্ভাব্য অস্ত্রোপচারের জন্য [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Surajmukhi Rural Hospital]]-এর জেনারেল সার্জারি বিভাগে পাঠানো হয়েছে। রোগীর [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] নথিপত্রের জন্য সংযুক্ত করা হয়েছে। কোনো অতিরিক্ত তথ্য বা অস্ত্রোপচারের সময় নির্ধারণের জন্য অনুগ্রহ করে রোগীর স্ত্রীর সাথে [[PHONE_NUMBER|9832147650]]-এ যোগাযোগ করুন, যার নাম [[RELATIVE_NAME|Sushila Mandal]]। শনাক্তকরণের উদ্দেশ্যে রোগীর [[DRIVING_LICENCE|WB04 20100012345]] এবং [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|P1234567]] প্রদান করা হয়েছে।
generated_text_en:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-01]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Surajmukhi Rural Hospital]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Subrata Chatterjee]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|Pradip Mandal]], [[AGE|60]] / [[GENDER|Male]], District [[DISTRICT|Birbhum]]
Reason: Patient presents with a painful, irreducible right inguinal hernia of 6 months duration, requiring surgical intervention.

Patient is a 60-year-old retired male from Birbhum, West Bengal, currently married. He is a Bengali speaker with functional illiteracy. The patient reports a gradual increase in the size of a right-sided groin lump over the past six months. The lump is more prominent when he stands or strains and has become increasingly painful over the last month. He denies any history of trauma to the area. There is no associated fever, chills, or change in bowel habits. He reports occasional constipation.

On examination, a 6x4 cm, tender, non-reducible mass is palpable in the right inguinal region, extending towards the scrotum. The overlying skin is normal in color and temperature. There are no signs of bowel obstruction. The rest of the abdominal examination is unremarkable.

Diagnosis: Right Inguinal Hernia, likely incarcerated.

Plan: The patient is referred to the Department of General Surgery at [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Surajmukhi Rural Hospital]] for evaluation and possible surgical repair. The patient's [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] is attached for records. Please contact the patient's spouse, [[RELATIVE_NAME|Sushila Mandal]], at [[PHONE_NUMBER|9832147650]] for any further information or to schedule the procedure. For identification purposes, the patient's [[DRIVING_LICENCE|WB04 20100012345]] and [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|P1234567]] are provided.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

professional_persona: Sumit Sutradhar, a diligent packer at a regional logistics centre, combines hands‑on dexterity with a practical curiosity for better packing methods, they reliably meet daily shipment targets while quietly studying short videos to improve workflow.

sports_persona: Sumit Sutradhar enjoys playing casual kabaddi on the village ground and follows the Kolkata Knight Riders in IPL as well as Mohun Bagan’s football matches, they attend local tournaments when free and keep a modest fitness routine by walking through the paddy fields each evening.
arts_persona: Sumit Sutradhar regularly tunes into folk songs on the local radio, especially songs by Abbas Uddin and modern singer Anupam Roy, they also enjoy watching folk‑drama Jatra performances during festivals, which enrich their cultural palate.
travel_persona: Sumit Sutradhar likes weekend trips to the Sundarbans mangrove forests and occasional pilgrimages to Gangasagar, they usually travel by shared bus or mahogany boat, preferring modest experiences over luxury stays.
culinary_persona: Sumit Sutradhar delights in cooking simple regional dishes like aloo posto with poppy seeds and shukto with bitter gourds, they often share these home‑cooked meals with neighbours, preferring mustard oil and green chilies for flavor.
persona: Sumit Sutradhar is a 30‑year‑old packer who blends hands‑on skill with a curiosity for small innovations, they juggle village traditions, modest savings, and a lingering back ache while dreaming of supervising a team.
cultural_background: Sumit belongs to a Bengali rural community in South 24 Parganas, West Bengal. His cultural life is shaped by agrarian rhythms, seasonal festivals such as Durga Puja, Raja, and local village rituals honoring deities like Shitala. He grew up in a joint family where respect for elders, communal cooperation, and simple folk customs are valued. While his upbringing emphasizes tradition and practicality, his innate curiosity leads him to appreciate modest innovations—like observing new packing methods in nearby markets—and to blend them with established ways.
linguistic_background: Sumit’s native language is Bengali, which he speaks fluently and uses for daily communication with family and neighbours. He also understands spoken English well enough to follow simple instructions at work and to interact with supervisors, though he cannot read or write in either language due to his illiteracy. His functional literacy is limited to recognizing common symbols, numerals, and basic signboards, which he relies on for tasks like counting packages or reading labels.
skills_and_expertise: Sumit has developed strong manual dexterity through years of packing work, including efficient box handling, stacking, and labeling. He is proficient in basic inventory tracking using visual cues, and can quickly count and organize up to dozens of packages. His physical stamina enables him to work long shifts under varying weather conditions. He possesses basic arithmetic skills for tallying items, time management to meet shipping deadlines, and the ability to coordinate with fellow workers to ensure smooth workflow. Additionally, his curiosity drives him to observe and adopt small improvements in packing techniques.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Manual packaging', 'Box handling and stacking', 'Basic inventory tracking', 'Physical stamina', 'Basic arithmetic', 'Time management', 'Team coordination', 'Observational learning of new techniques']
hobbies_and_interests: Sumit enjoys listening to Bengali folk songs on the local radio, playing casual kabaddi with his friends in the village ground, and taking long walks through the paddy fields during evenings. He takes pleasure in helping organize community festivals, especially setting up temporary stalls and handling decorations. His curiosity also leads him to watch short videos about packaging tricks on his mobile phone whenever he gets a chance. Additionally, he likes cooking simple Bengali dishes like aloo posto and sharing them with neighbours.

career_goals_and_ambitions: As a 30-year-old packer, Sumit aspires to move up the occupational ladder within the logistics sector. He aims to become a shift supervisor or a foreman, overseeing a small team and managing daily packing schedules. Recognizing the limitations of his illiteracy, he wishes to enroll in a basic adult literacy program to acquire reading and writing skills, which he believes will open doors to better responsibilities. He also hopes to adopt more efficient packing methods, perhaps by learning from nearby warehouses, and eventually start a modest home‑based packaging service catering to local artisans. These goals reflect his blend of curiosity for improvement and practical desire for stable earnings to support a future family.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Discharge Summary for clinical domain Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Bengali (code=bn, script=Bengali). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=West Bengal, district=South Twenty Four Parganas.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Bengali for Bengali.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 30
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: Packer, Hand
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: West Bengal
- district: South Twenty Four Parganas
- first_language: Bengali
- clinical_persona_summary: A 30‑year‑old male from rural South 24 Parganas, West Bengal, with a packer occupation and illiterate education, speaks Bengali. He is never married and lives in a rural zone. His lifestyle includes a back ache from manual labor and a modest savings goal. He follows local festivals and uses simple English for work.

Document format guide for Discharge Summary (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Discharge Summary — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] DOB [[DOB|1994-06-21]] [[AGE|30]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|...]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]] | Course / advice: <concise paragraphs matching domain + persona sex/age>

MANDATORY inline entities (14 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ADMISSION_NUMBER — Admission Number e.g. [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- ABHA_ADDRESS — ABHA Address e.g. [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
- CASTE — Caste e.g. [[CASTE|OBC]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ডিসচার্জ সামারি — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|South 24 Parganas District Hospital]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Sanjay Kumar]] জন্ম তারিখ [[DOB|1994-08-15]] [[AGE|30]] [[GENDER|Male]]
ভর্তি [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]] ওয়ার্ড [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]] বেড [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
ডাক্তার [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Amitava Chatterjee]] | কোর্স / পরামর্শ: রোগীর দীর্ঘস্থায়ী কাশি, মৃদু জ্বর এবং গত দুই মাস ধরে উল্লেখযোগ্য ওজন হ্রাসের ইতিহাস নিয়ে ভর্তি করা হয়েছিল। কফ স্মিয়ার মাইক্রোস্কোপি এবং GeneXpert MTB/RIF অ্যাসে সহ বিভিন্ন পরীক্ষা-নিরীক্ষায় পালমোনারি যক্ষ্মা নিশ্চিত করা হয়েছে। তাকে আইসোনিয়াজিড, রিফাম্পিসিন, পাইরাজিনামাইড এবং ইথাম্বুটল সহ সংশোধিত জাতীয় যক্ষ্মা নিয়ন্ত্রণ কর্মসূচি (RNTCP) অনুযায়ী চিকিৎসা শুরু করা হয়েছিল। তার রক্তাল্পতা বা অ্যানিমিয়া পরীক্ষা করা হয়েছিল এবং আয়রনের অভাব পাওয়া গিয়েছিল, যার জন্য তাকে আয়রন এবং ফলিক অ্যাসিডের ট্যাবলেট দেওয়া হয়েছে। রোগীকে চিকিৎসার প্রতি আনুগত্য, পুরো ছয় মাসের কোর্স সম্পন্ন করার গুরুত্ব এবং ওষুধের সম্ভাব্য পার্শ্বপ্রতিক্রিয়া সম্পর্কে বিস্তারিত পরামর্শ দেওয়া হয়েছে। তাকে তার গ্রামের ডি.ও.টি.এস (DOTS) সেন্টারে প্রতিদিনের তত্ত্বাবধানে থেরাপির জন্য ফলো-আপ করার পরামর্শ দেওয়া হয়েছে। রোগীর বাবা, [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Kumar]], পরামর্শের সময় উপস্থিত ছিলেন এবং রোগীর চিকিৎসায় সহায়তা করবেন। তাকে স্থিতিশীল অবস্থায় ডিসচার্জ করা হয়েছে এবং কোনো গুরুতর ওষুধের পার্শ্বপ্রতিক্রিয়া বা উপসর্গ বৃদ্ধি পেলে অবিলম্বে ফিরে আসার পরামর্শ দেওয়া হয়েছে।

[[DISTRICT|South Twenty Four Parganas]]
[[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
[[ABHA_ADDRESS|sanjay.kumar.wb@abdm]]
[[CASTE|SC]]

professional_persona: Sumit Sutradhar, a diligent packer at a regional logistics centre, combines hands‑on dexterity with a practical curiosity for better packing methods, they reliably meet daily shipment targets while quietly studying short videos to improve workflow.
linguistic_persona: Sumit Sutradhar speaks fluent his native regional language and understands spoken English well enough to follow instructions at work, they rely on visual cues and numerals for counting packages because they cannot read or write in either language.
sports_persona: Sumit Sutradhar enjoys playing casual kabaddi on the village ground and follows the Kolkata Knight Riders in IPL as well as Mohun Bagan’s football matches, they attend local tournaments when free and keep a modest fitness routine by walking through the paddy fields each evening.
arts_persona: Sumit Sutradhar regularly tunes into folk songs on the local radio, especially songs by Abbas Uddin and modern singer Anupam Roy, they also enjoy watching folk‑drama Jatra performances during festivals, which enrich their cultural palate.
travel_persona: Sumit Sutradhar likes weekend trips to the Sundarbans mangrove forests and occasional pilgrimages to Gangasagar, they usually travel by shared bus or mahogany boat, preferring modest experiences over luxury stays.
culinary_persona: Sumit Sutradhar delights in cooking simple regional dishes like aloo posto with poppy seeds and shukto with bitter gourds, they often share these home‑cooked meals with neighbours, preferring mustard oil and green chilies for flavor.
persona: Sumit Sutradhar is a 30‑year‑old packer who blends hands‑on skill with a curiosity for small innovations, they juggle village traditions, modest savings, and a lingering back ache while dreaming of supervising a team.
cultural_background: Sumit belongs to a Bengali rural community in South 24 Parganas, West Bengal. His cultural life is shaped by agrarian rhythms, seasonal festivals such as Durga Puja, Raja, and local village rituals honoring deities like Shitala. He grew up in a joint family where respect for elders, communal cooperation, and simple folk customs are valued. While his upbringing emphasizes tradition and practicality, his innate curiosity leads him to appreciate modest innovations—like observing new packing methods in nearby markets—and to blend them with established ways.
linguistic_background: Sumit’s native language is Bengali, which he speaks fluently and uses for daily communication with family and neighbours. He also understands spoken English well enough to follow simple instructions at work and to interact with supervisors, though he cannot read or write in either language due to his illiteracy. His functional literacy is limited to recognizing common symbols, numerals, and basic signboards, which he relies on for tasks like counting packages or reading labels.
skills_and_expertise: Sumit has developed strong manual dexterity through years of packing work, including efficient box handling, stacking, and labeling. He is proficient in basic inventory tracking using visual cues, and can quickly count and organize up to dozens of packages. His physical stamina enables him to work long shifts under varying weather conditions. He possesses basic arithmetic skills for tallying items, time management to meet shipping deadlines, and the ability to coordinate with fellow workers to ensure smooth workflow. Additionally, his curiosity drives him to observe and adopt small improvements in packing techniques.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Manual packaging', 'Box handling and stacking', 'Basic inventory tracking', 'Physical stamina', 'Basic arithmetic', 'Time management', 'Team coordination', 'Observational learning of new techniques']
hobbies_and_interests: Sumit enjoys listening to Bengali folk songs on the local radio, playing casual kabaddi with his friends in the village ground, and taking long walks through the paddy fields during evenings. He takes pleasure in helping organize community festivals, especially setting up temporary stalls and handling decorations. His curiosity also leads him to watch short videos about packaging tricks on his mobile phone whenever he gets a chance. Additionally, he likes cooking simple Bengali dishes like aloo posto and sharing them with neighbours.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to Bengali folk music on radio', 'Playing casual kabaddi with friends', 'Evening walks through paddy fields', 'Helping organize community festivals', 'Watching packaging trick videos on mobile', 'Cooking simple Bengali dishes']
career_goals_and_ambitions: As a 30-year-old packer, Sumit aspires to move up the occupational ladder within the logistics sector. He aims to become a shift supervisor or a foreman, overseeing a small team and managing daily packing schedules. Recognizing the limitations of his illiteracy, he wishes to enroll in a basic adult literacy program to acquire reading and writing skills, which he believes will open doors to better responsibilities. He also hopes to adopt more efficient packing methods, perhaps by learning from nearby warehouses, and eventually start a modest home‑based packaging service catering to local artisans. These goals reflect his blend of curiosity for improvement and practical desire for stable earnings to support a future family.
persona_clinical_summary: A 30-year-old male from rural South 24 Parganas, West Bengal, works as a packer with limited formal education and speaks Bengali as his primary language. He is never married and has no reported sports, travel, or culinary interests, though he may experience back pain from manual labor. His lifestyle is shaped by agrarian rhythms and modest savings, with no documented health behaviors beyond basic work-related activities.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.

বিষয়: [[PATIENT_NAME|Sanjay Kumar]], [[AGE|30]] / [[GENDER|Male]], জেলা [[DISTRICT|South Twenty Four Parganas]]

কারণ: দীর্ঘস্থায়ী কোমরের নিচের দিকে ব্যথা এবং ডান পায়ের দিকে ছড়িয়ে পড়া ব্যথা এবং মাঝে মাঝে অসাড়তা, গত তিন মাস ধরে, সন্দেহভাজন লম্বার ডিস্ক প্রোল্যাপস।

রোগী সঞ্জয় কুমার, 30 বছর বয়সী পুরুষ প্যাকার, গ্রামীণ দক্ষিণ চব্বিশ পরগনা থেকে এসেছেন, তাঁর কোমরের নিচের দিকে ব্যথার ইতিহাস রয়েছে। ব্যথাকে একটি মৃদু ব্যথা হিসেবে বর্ণনা করা হয়েছে, যা ধীরে ধীরে শুরু হয়েছিল এবং গত দুই মাস ধরে ক্রমাগত বাড়ছে। তিন সপ্তাহ আগে, তাঁর ডান পায়ের পেছনের দিকে গোড়ালি পর্যন্ত তীব্র, ঝিনঝিন করা ব্যথা শুরু হয়েছে, যার সাথে একই পায়ের বাইরের দিকে মাঝে মাঝে অসাড়তা রয়েছে। দীর্ঘক্ষণ দাঁড়িয়ে থাকা, সামনের দিকে ঝুঁকে থাকা এবং ভারী বস্তু তোলা, যা তাঁর দৈনন্দিন কাজের অংশ, এই ব্যথার তীব্রতা বাড়িয়ে দেয়। তিনি কোনো উল্লেখযোগ্য আঘাত, জ্বর, ওজন হ্রাস বা অন্ত্র/মূত্রাশয়ের কোনো সমস্যার কথা জানাননি।

পরীক্ষায় দেখা গেছে, রোগী সচেতন এবং স্বাভাবিক। তাঁর একটি মৃদু অ্যান্টিলজিক গেট (ব্যথা এড়ানোর জন্য হাঁটার ধরন) রয়েছে। মেরুদণ্ডের পরীক্ষায় L4-L5 এবং L5-S1 ইন্টারস্পাইনাস স্পেসের উপরে স্পর্শকাতরতা দেখা গেছে। ডান দিকে স্ট্রেইট লেগ রেইজ টেস্ট 45 ডিগ্রিতে পজিটিভ এসেছে, যা তাঁর পায়ের ব্যথার পুনরাবৃত্তি ঘটাচ্ছে। ডান পায়ের অ্যাঙ্কেল ডরসিফ্লেক্সর এবং বড় পায়ের আঙুলের এক্সটেনসরে পেশীর শক্তি 4/5। সংবেদনশীল পরীক্ষায় ডান পায়ের উপরিভাগ এবং ডান পায়ের বাইরের দিকে অনুভূতি কমে যাওয়ার কথা জানা গেছে। রিফ্লেক্স স্বাভাবিক।

অস্থায়ী রোগনির্ণয় হলো সন্দেহভাজন L4-L5 ডিস্ক প্রোল্যাপসের কারণে ডান দিকের L5 র‍্যাডিকুলোপ্যাথি।

জেলা হাসপাতালে করা তদন্তের মধ্যে রয়েছে লম্বার স্পাইনের এম.আর.আই, যা এখনও বাকি আছে। সি.বি.সি, ই.এস.আর এবং সিরাম ইলেকট্রোলাইটসহ প্রাথমিক রক্ত পরীক্ষা স্বাভাবিক সীমার মধ্যে রয়েছে।

ক্লিনিকাল ফলাফল এবং রোগীর পেশার কথা বিবেচনা করে, যেখানে ভারী শারীরিক পরিশ্রম জড়িত, আরও মূল্যায়ন এবং সম্ভাব্য অস্ত্রোপচারের পরামর্শের জন্য [[HOSPITAL_NAME|SSKM Hospital, Kolkata]]-এর অর্থোপেডিক বিভাগে রেফার করার পরামর্শ দেওয়া হচ্ছে। রোগীর [[MRN|MRN-WB-2024-0815-001]] এবং [[PHONE_NUMBER|9830055544]] নথিপত্রের জন্য প্রদান করা হয়েছে।

আরও কোনো তথ্যের জন্য, অনুগ্রহ করে রেফারকারী চিকিৎসকের সাথে যোগাযোগ করুন।

[[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Kumar]] রোগীর বড় ভাই এবং সাথে আসা আত্মীয়।

[[PASSPORT_NUMBER|P7894561]] এবং [[DRIVING_LICENCE|WB04 20100001234]] পরিচয়পত্রের জন্য উপলব্ধ।
generated_text_en:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-WB-2024-0815]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|South 24 Parganas District Hospital]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Subrata Chatterjee]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|Sanjay Kumar]], [[AGE|30]] / [[GENDER|Male]], District [[DISTRICT|South Twenty Four Parganas]]
Reason: Chronic low back pain with radiating right leg pain and intermittent numbness for the past three months, suspected lumbar disc prolapse.

Patient Sanjay Kumar, a 30-year-old male packer from rural South Twenty Four Parganas, presents with a history of progressive low back pain. The pain, described as a dull ache, began insidiously and has become constant over the last two months. Three weeks ago, he developed sharp, shooting pain radiating down the posterior aspect of his right leg to the foot, associated with intermittent numbness in the lateral aspect of the same foot. The pain is exacerbated by prolonged standing, bending, and lifting heavy objects, which are part of his daily occupation. He reports no significant trauma, fever, weight loss, or bowel/bladder dysfunction.

On examination, the patient is alert and oriented. He has a mild antalgic gait. Spinal examination reveals tenderness over the L4-L5 and L5-S1 interspinous spaces. Straight leg raise test on the right side is positive at 45 degrees, reproducing his radiating leg pain. Motor power is 4/5 in the right ankle dorsiflexors and great toe extensors. Sensory examination shows decreased sensation over the dorsum of the right foot and the lateral aspect of the right leg. Reflexes are normal.

Provisional diagnosis is right-sided L5 radiculopathy secondary to a suspected L4-L5 disc prolapse.

Investigations ordered at the district hospital include an MRI of the lumbar spine, which is pending. Basic blood work including CBC, ESR, and serum electrolytes are within normal limits.

Given the clinical findings and the patient's occupation, which involves heavy manual labor, a referral to the Department of Orthopaedics at [[HOSPITAL_NAME|SSKM Hospital, Kolkata]] is recommended for further evaluation and potential surgical consultation. The patient's [[MRN|MRN-WB-2024-0815-001]] and [[PHONE_NUMBER|9830055544]] are provided for records.

For any further information, please contact the referring physician.

[[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Kumar]] is the patient's elder brother and accompanying relative.

[[PASSPORT_NUMBER|P7894561]] and [[DRIVING_LICENCE|WB04 20100001234]] are available for identification.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Insurance Claim / TPA Form for clinical domain Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Bodo (code=brx, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Assam, district=Baksa.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Bodo.

PLACEMENT (vehicle): Put VEHICLE_REGISTRATION exactly once (parking/ambulance or motor claim line). Do not dump or repeat it.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 24
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: Labeller
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Assam
- district: Baksa
- first_language: Bodo
- clinical_persona_summary: A 24-year-old male from Assam’s Baksa district, never married, works as a labeler with an illiterate education level. He resides in a rural zone and speaks Bodo as his first language, with limited literacy. Clinically, he exhibits a laid-back demeanor with occasional worry about future prospects, and engages in community-oriented activities like folk dance and informal football. His lifestyle includes a preference for oral communication and limited engagement with formal health-related systems.

Document format guide for Insurance Claim / TPA Form (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
TPA claim — Policy [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|25]] [[GENDER|Male]] Aadhaar [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]

PAN [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]] IFSC [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]] account [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|...]]

MANDATORY inline entities (16 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.

रोगीयाव थाम हप्तासिम अरायथा खोख्लैनाय, खम बिदुं आरो ओजोन खम जानायनि लक्षण नुजादों। थुख्रि आनजादाव एसिड-फास्ट बेसिली (acid-fast bacilli) मोननाय जादों। चेस्ट X-ray आव गोजौ बाहागोनि इनफिल्ट्रेट्स (upper lobe infiltrates) नुनानै थार खालामनाय जादों। बिथांखौ पल्मोनारि टिबि (pulmonary tuberculosis) नि थाखाय केटेगोरि I DOTS थेरापि जागायनाय जादों। बिथांनाव हाइपरटेन्सन (hypertension) निबो जारिमिन दं, जायनि थाखाय बिथांखौ सानफ्रोमबो 5mg एमलोडिपिन (Amlodipine 5mg) लिरनानै होनाय जादों। रोगीखौ नै हप्तानि उनाव थुख्रि फिन-आनजाद खालामनो आरो हाइपरटेन्सननि मुलि सोलिबाय थानो थाखाय सुबुरुन होनाय जादों। गिबि नै दाननि टिबि-विरोधी मुलि आरो आनजादफोरनि गासै बेसेना प्राय Rs. 15,000 जागोन। रोगीनि ASHA मावग्रा सुनित बोरोआ (Sunita Boro) बेखौ दैथायहरनायाव मदद खालामदों आरो बिथाङा फाहामथायनि नेम मानिनायाव मदद खालामगोन। रोगीया बाकसा जिलानि गोरोइमारी गामियाव थायो। बिथांनि सोमोन्दोगोनां बिरेन बोरोआ (Biren Boro) फाहामथाय आरो इन्सुरेन्स क्लेमनि थाखाय गनायथि होदों। बे क्लेमखौ गोजौआव मख'नाय खरसाफोरनि रां मोनफिननायनि थाखाय जमा होनाय जादों।

Patient presented with persistent cough for three weeks, low-grade fever, and weight loss. Sputum examination showed acid-fast bacilli. Chest X-ray confirmed upper lobe infiltrates. He was started on Category I DOTS therapy for pulmonary tuberculosis. He also has a history of hypertension, for which he was prescribed Amlodipine 5mg daily. The patient was advised to follow up in two weeks for sputum re-evaluation and to continue his anti-hypertensive medication. The total estimated cost for the initial two-month anti-tubercular regimen and investigations is approximately Rs. 15,000. The patient's ASHA worker, Sunita Boro, facilitated the referral and will assist with treatment adherence. The patient resides in the village of Goroimari, Baksa district. His relative, Mr. Biren Boro, provided consent for the treatment and insurance claim. The claim is submitted for reimbursement of the above-mentioned expenses.
judge_reasoning: Bodo Devanagari prose correct; all TYPEs allow-listed; Latin clinical terms permitted; persona/domain fit; no mismatches or length issues.

professional_persona: Monobara Khatun is a budding home‑based entrepreneur who combines her expertise in hand‑loom weaving of traditional mekhela textiles and small‑batch pickling with meticulous budgeting and community event organization, using her curiosity to experiment with designs while staying grounded in proven methods.
linguistic_persona: Monobara Khatun speaks Bodo as her mother tongue, can read and write a few basic words in Devanagari, communicates in conversational Assamese for daily interactions, and has only rudimentary exposure to Hindi and English.
sports_persona: Monobara Khatun keeps fit by gardening and occasional walks, enjoys watching NorthEast United FC in the Indian Super League, participates in village kabaddi games during festivals, and is a member of the local women's cricket group that meets on Sundays.
arts_persona: Monobara Khatun finds joy in listening to regional folk songs such as 'Bwisaguni' and Assamese tunes by Zubeen Garg, expresses creativity through hand‑loom weaving of mekhela and crocheting colourful scarves, and regularly visits local craft fairs where she admires the work of artisans like Jatin Das and Rima Saikia.
travel_persona: Monobara Khatun enjoys short rural getaways to nearby tea gardens in Jorhat, dreams of visiting the historic Kamakhya Temple in Guwahati and the pristine beaches of Goa, plans trips carefully to stay within her modest budget, and prefers traveling with close friends or family rather than large tours.
culinary_persona: Monobara Khatun excels in traditional cooking, especially dishes like ooti (steamed rice cake) and mansi (spicy fish curry), delights in making tangy homemade pickles with mustard seeds and green chilies, and prefers the earthy flavors of bamboo shoots and local herbs.
persona: Monobara Khatun is a diligent 29‑year‑old who balances her love for traditional weaving and pickling with a habit of over‑thinking every decision, often turning to journaling and herbal teas to calm her nerves.
cultural_background: Monobara belongs to the Bodo community, an indigenous tribal group in Assam's Kokrajhar district. Growing up in a rural setting, her life is shaped by Bodo customs, agrarian rhythms and Muslim faith, resulting in a blend of tribal traditions, Assamese rural culture and Islamic practices. She participates in Bodo festivals like Bwisagu, while also observing Ramadan and Eid with her family. The community's emphasis on collective harmony and modest living influences her daily routines.
linguistic_background: Monobara's mother tongue is Bodo, which she uses for everyday communication at home and in the village. Because she left school before primary level, her formal literacy is limited; she can recognize and write a few basic Bodo words using the Devanagari script, but reading longer texts is challenging. She has picked up conversational Assamese from neighbours and market interactions, though she does not read or write it proficiently. She has no formal training in Hindi or English, so her proficiency in those languages is minimal.
skills_and_expertise: Monobara excels at traditional Bodo cooking, preparing dishes like ooti and mansi, knows handloom weaving of traditional mekhela items, manages household finances on a tight budget, takes care of children and the elderly, organizes small community events during religious festivals, and has practical knowledge of herbal remedies used in rural Assam.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Traditional Bodo cooking", "Handloom weaving of mekhela", "Household budgeting", "Childcare and eldercare", "Community event organization", "Herbal remedy knowledge"]
hobbies_and_interests: Monobara enjoys listening to Bodo folk songs, especially those sung during Bwisagu; she likes gardening, tending to a small vegetable patch; she reads religious pamphlets in Arabic script when available; she practices simple meditation and journaling to cope with stress; she occasionally attends local craft fairs; she likes crocheting scarves for family.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Listening to Bodo folk music", "Vegetable gardening", "Reading religious pamphlets in Arabic script", "Simple meditation and journaling", "Attending local craft fairs", "Crocheting scarves for family"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Monobara aspires to augment her family's livelihood by setting up a modest home‑based venture producing hand‑woven Bodo textiles and homemade pickles, which she hopes to sell at local markets and to tourists visiting Assam. She also wishes to acquire basic tailoring skills to diversify her crafts. On a personal level she aims to manage stress better by attending community health workshops, and she hopes to become a trusted resource for other women in her village, offering guidance on nutrition, child care, and modest religious education.
persona_clinical_summary: A 29-year-old female from Assam's Kokrajhar district, currently married and residing in a rural area, speaks Bodo as her first language. She has below-primary education and is a homemaker. Her lifestyle includes traditional Bodo customs, limited formal literacy, and a preference for herbal teas and journaling.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER
