professional_persona: Monobara Khatun is a budding home‑based entrepreneur who combines her expertise in hand‑loom weaving of traditional mekhela textiles and small‑batch pickling with meticulous budgeting and community event organization, using her curiosity to experiment with designs while staying grounded in proven methods.
linguistic_persona: Monobara Khatun speaks Bodo as her mother tongue, can read and write a few basic words in Devanagari, communicates in conversational Assamese for daily interactions, and has only rudimentary exposure to Hindi and English.
sports_persona: Monobara Khatun keeps fit by gardening and occasional walks, enjoys watching NorthEast United FC in the Indian Super League, participates in village kabaddi games during festivals, and is a member of the local women's cricket group that meets on Sundays.
arts_persona: Monobara Khatun finds joy in listening to regional folk songs such as 'Bwisaguni' and Assamese tunes by Zubeen Garg, expresses creativity through hand‑loom weaving of mekhela and crocheting colourful scarves, and regularly visits local craft fairs where she admires the work of artisans like Jatin Das and Rima Saikia.
travel_persona: Monobara Khatun enjoys short rural getaways to nearby tea gardens in Jorhat, dreams of visiting the historic Kamakhya Temple in Guwahati and the pristine beaches of Goa, plans trips carefully to stay within her modest budget, and prefers traveling with close friends or family rather than large tours.
culinary_persona: Monobara Khatun excels in traditional cooking, especially dishes like ooti (steamed rice cake) and mansi (spicy fish curry), delights in making tangy homemade pickles with mustard seeds and green chilies, and prefers the earthy flavors of bamboo shoots and local herbs.
persona: Monobara Khatun is a diligent 29‑year‑old who balances her love for traditional weaving and pickling with a habit of over‑thinking every decision, often turning to journaling and herbal teas to calm her nerves.
cultural_background: Monobara belongs to the Bodo community, an indigenous tribal group in Assam's Kokrajhar district. Growing up in a rural setting, her life is shaped by Bodo customs, agrarian rhythms and Muslim faith, resulting in a blend of tribal traditions, Assamese rural culture and Islamic practices. She participates in Bodo festivals like Bwisagu, while also observing Ramadan and Eid with her family. The community's emphasis on collective harmony and modest living influences her daily routines.
linguistic_background: Monobara's mother tongue is Bodo, which she uses for everyday communication at home and in the village. Because she left school before primary level, her formal literacy is limited; she can recognize and write a few basic Bodo words using the Devanagari script, but reading longer texts is challenging. She has picked up conversational Assamese from neighbours and market interactions, though she does not read or write it proficiently. She has no formal training in Hindi or English, so her proficiency in those languages is minimal.
skills_and_expertise: Monobara excels at traditional Bodo cooking, preparing dishes like ooti and mansi, knows handloom weaving of traditional mekhela items, manages household finances on a tight budget, takes care of children and the elderly, organizes small community events during religious festivals, and has practical knowledge of herbal remedies used in rural Assam.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Traditional Bodo cooking", "Handloom weaving of mekhela", "Household budgeting", "Childcare and eldercare", "Community event organization", "Herbal remedy knowledge"]
hobbies_and_interests: Monobara enjoys listening to Bodo folk songs, especially those sung during Bwisagu; she likes gardening, tending to a small vegetable patch; she reads religious pamphlets in Arabic script when available; she practices simple meditation and journaling to cope with stress; she occasionally attends local craft fairs; she likes crocheting scarves for family.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Listening to Bodo folk music", "Vegetable gardening", "Reading religious pamphlets in Arabic script", "Simple meditation and journaling", "Attending local craft fairs", "Crocheting scarves for family"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Monobara aspires to augment her family's livelihood by setting up a modest home‑based venture producing hand‑woven Bodo textiles and homemade pickles, which she hopes to sell at local markets and to tourists visiting Assam. She also wishes to acquire basic tailoring skills to diversify her crafts. On a personal level she aims to manage stress better by attending community health workshops, and she hopes to become a trusted resource for other women in her village, offering guidance on nutrition, child care, and modest religious education.
persona_clinical_summary: A 29-year-old female from rural Kokrajhar, Assam, who is married and retired, speaks Bodo as her first language and has below-primary education. She follows a Muslim faith and her lifestyle includes traditional home-based activities like hand-loom weaving and pickling. Her daily routine is shaped by rural agrarian rhythms and limited formal literacy.

You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

persona: Ranu Devi blends meticulous herbal expertise with a warm, community‑focused spirit, yet often worries about finances and health, making her both a reliable caregiver and a cautious planner.

Living in the rural hills of Reasi, her daily life is intertwined with traditional customs, seasonal agricultural cycles, and community gatherings that celebrate Hindu festivals like Navratri, Diwali, and local fairs where herbal knowledge is exchanged.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:

ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Jammu & Kashmir, district=Reasi.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Dogri.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):

She speaks Dogri at home and Hindi for basic literacy, and lives in a rural setting. Clinically, she may have financial and health concerns, with a cautious approach to planning.

Document format guide for Referral Letter (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:

Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|36]] / [[GENDER|Male]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]

- PASSPORT_NUMBER — Passport Number e.g. [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
- DRIVING_LICENCE — Driving Licence Number e.g. [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Sub-District Hospital Reasi]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Kumar]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|Smt. Rinki Devi]], [[AGE|37]] / [[GENDER|Female]], District [[DISTRICT|Reasi]]
Reason: Persistent right upper quadrant abdominal pain with associated vomiting, suspected acute cholecystitis.

Patient Smt. Rinki Devi, a 37-year-old female gatherer of medicinal herbs from a rural area in Reasi district, presents with a history of intermittent right upper quadrant pain for the past three weeks. The pain has become constant and severe over the last 48 hours, radiating to her back. She reports associated nausea and two episodes of non-bilious vomiting yesterday. The patient denies any fever, jaundice, or changes in bowel habits. Her last menstrual period was 15 days ago. She has no known prior surgeries. On examination, she is afebrile, with a heart rate of 95 bpm, blood pressure of 110/70 mmHg, and a tender right upper quadrant with guarding and a positive Murphy's sign.

Given the clinical picture suggestive of acute cholecystitis, the patient requires further evaluation and management. An ultrasound abdomen has been ordered and shows gallstones with gallbladder wall thickening. The patient is advised to proceed to a tertiary care center for possible surgical intervention.

For further management and potential cholecystectomy, the patient is referred to the Department of General Surgery at [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Government Medical College Jammu]]. The patient's [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] is attached for reference. The patient's husband, [[RELATIVE_NAME|Sh. Manoj Kumar]], has been informed and is accompanying her.

Contact for any queries: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9419876543]].

[[PASSPORT_NUMBER|J1234567]]
[[DRIVING_LICENCE|JK01 20100012345]]

linguistic_persona: Ranu Devi speaks Dogri fluently, reads and writes Hindi with functional literacy, and manages basic English conversations, which helps them negotiate with visiting health workers and tourists while carefully noting herb catalogues.
sports_persona: Ranu Devi stays active by playing cricket with her village team on weekends, cheering for Jammu and Kashmir FC in the I‑League, joining occasional Kabaddi matches during community fairs, and taking daily hill walks to scout for new herbs.
arts_persona: Ranu Devi delights in singing traditional Dogri folk songs, drawing inspiration from legendary folk vocalist Rajinder Raghav, while also enjoying the timeless melodies of Lata Mangeshkar, the soulful ghazals of Jagjit Singh, and participating in local drama performances that blend music and storytelling.
travel_persona: Ranu Devi enjoys weekend trips to the hills of Himachal, occasional pilgrimages to the revered mountain shrine near Vaishno Devi, and annual family vacations to the coastal beaches of Goa, balancing her love for nature with a limited family budget and responsibilities.
culinary_persona: Ranu Devi loves the hearty flavours of rajma, dum aloo, and madra, and enjoys adding fresh coriander, mustard seeds, and dried fenugreek leaves to her dishes, though she rarely cooks for herself and prefers sharing meals with neighbours during festivals.
persona: Ranu Devi blends meticulous herbal expertise with a warm, community‑focused spirit, yet often worries about finances and health, making her both a reliable caregiver and a cautious planner.
cultural_background: Ranu belongs to the Dogra community of the Jammu region, a culturally rich group known for its folk music, vibrant festivals, and a deep respect for nature. Living in the rural hills of Reasi, her daily life is intertwined with traditional customs, seasonal agricultural cycles, and community gatherings that celebrate Hindu festivals like Navratri, Diwali, and local fairs where herbal knowledge is exchanged.
linguistic_background: Ranu's mother tongue is Dogri, which she speaks fluently and uses daily for household communication and herbal trade. She is also proficient in Hindi, the state language, allowing her to read and write basic documents, including herb catalogues and health pamphlets. Her primary education equips her with functional literacy in Hindi and Devanagari script, and she has a practical working knowledge of English, sufficient for simple conversations with visiting health workers and tourists.
skills_and_expertise: Ranu has an in-depth knowledge of the local flora and is adept at identifying and harvesting medicinal herbs such as tulsi, giloy, and ashwagandha. She prepares traditional remedies, including decoctions, powders, and herbal oils, and provides basic first-aid services using these preparations. Her organisational skills enable her to coordinate group collection trips, maintain simple inventories, and document herb uses for community reference.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Herbal Identification", "Medicinal Preparation", "First Aid", "Community Coordination", "Traditional Knowledge Documentation"]
hobbies_and_interests: Ranu enjoys singing traditional Dogra folk songs, cooking regional dishes like rajma and dum aloo, and taking long walks in the hills to scout for new herb species. She also loves crafting herbal sachets for personal use, participating in village drama groups, and reading simple health pamphlets in Hindi.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Folk Singing", "Traditional Cooking", "Hill Walking", "Herbal Crafting", "Community Drama"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Ranu aspires to formalise her herbal practice by establishing a small community clinic that offers affordable herbal remedies and health education. She aims to train younger women in sustainable herb collection and documentation, and hopes to partner with NGOs to promote conservation of medicinal plant habitats. Financially, she wants to achieve a stable income that supports her family while preserving her cultural heritage.

She is married, speaks Dogri at home, and lives in a rural setting. Her lifestyle includes community-focused herbal trade and occasional hill walks for sourcing remedies.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
  [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
  [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
  [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]
  [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]
  [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
  [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
  [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
  [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
  [[PIN|4829]]
  [[PIN_CODE|208001]]
  [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
  [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
  [[RELIGION|Hindu]]
  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Radiology / Imaging Report for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Dogri (code=doi, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Jammu & Kashmir, district=Reasi.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Dogri.

PLACEMENT: Include [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] as the reporting radiologist (e.g. Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]]) — do not leave the doctor name as untagged prose.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 37
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Gatherer, Medicinal Herbs
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: Jammu & Kashmir
- district: Reasi
- first_language: Dogri
- clinical_persona_summary: A 37-year-old female from Reasi, Jammu & Kashmir, works as a gatherer of medicinal herbs and has primary education. She is married, speaks Dogri at home, and lives in a rural setting. Her lifestyle includes community-focused herbal trade and occasional hill walks for sourcing remedies.

Document format guide for Radiology / Imaging Report (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Imaging report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] | [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|45]] [[GENDER|Female]]
MRN [[MRN|...]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|...]] | Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Findings: <radiology prose, untagged>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- ENCOUNTER_ID — Encounter / Visit ID e.g. [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text:
रेडियोलॉजी रिपोर्ट — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Reasi]] | [[PATIENT_NAME|Shabnam Begum]] [[AGE|37]] [[GENDER|Female]]
MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-0915-004]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-2024-0915-004]] | रिपोर्ट डॉ [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Sharma]] द्वारा दी गई
निष्कर्ष: 15 सितंबर 2024 को खब्बे हत्थ दा प्लेन रेडियोग्राफ कीता गेआ। रेडियोग्राफ च खब्बे हत्थ दी स्हेई अलाइनमेंट दस्सी गई ऐ ते कोई बी तीव्र अस्थि असामान्यता नेईं ऐ। मेटाकार्पल्स, फालेंजस, ते कार्पल्स सलामत न। फ्रैक्चर, विस्थापन, या लाइटिक लीजन दा कोई सबूत नेईं लब्भेआ गेआ ऐ। कोमल ऊतक तल (soft tissue planes) सुरक्षित न। पैह्‌लें दे अध्ययनें कन्नै तुलना उपलब्ध नेईं ऐ।
रिपोर्ट: खब्बे हत्थ दा रेडियोग्राफ सामान्य ऐ। कोई बी तीव्र फ्रैक्चर या विस्थापन नेईं दिक्खेआ गेआ ऐ। नैदानिक सह-संबंध (Clinical correlation) दी सलाह दित्ती जंदी ऐ।
[[DISTRICT|Reasi]]
[[PHONE_NUMBER|9419283745]]
[[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-JK-023]]
[[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
generated_text_en:
Radiology report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Reasi]] | [[PATIENT_NAME|Shabnam Begum]] [[AGE|37]] [[GENDER|Female]]
MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-0915-004]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-2024-0915-004]] | Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Sharma]]
Findings: Plain radiograph of the left hand was performed on 15 September 2024. The radiograph demonstrates a well-aligned left hand with no acute osseous abnormality. The metacarpals, phalanges, and carpals are intact. No evidence of fracture, dislocation, or lytic lesion is identified. The soft tissue planes are preserved. Comparison with prior studies is not available.
Report: The radiograph of the left hand is normal. No acute fracture or dislocation is seen. Clinical correlation is advised.
[[DISTRICT|Reasi]]
[[PHONE_NUMBER|9419283745]]
[[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-JK-023]]
[[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

professional_persona: Sunita Poli, a home‑based craft entrepreneur and aspiring early‑childhood caregiver, combines her expertise in household budgeting, hand‑weaving, and community event coordination, and they plan to launch a line of hand‑woven shawls while preparing to open a modest neighbourhood daycare.
linguistic_persona: Sunita Poli, fluent in Dogri and comfortable conversing in Hindi, reads and writes Dogri in Devanagari, understands everyday Hindi media, and they navigate basic English signs, making their multilingual communication rooted in regional languages.
sports_persona: Sunita Poli, an avid hill‑walker and occasional cricket fan, follows Real Kashmir FC’s matches and joins a local women’s walking group that meets twice a week for fitness and fresh air, and they find the routine grounding.
arts_persona: Sunita Poli, an avid listener of Dogri folk songs by Ravindra Singh and a fan of Bollywood classics starring Shah Rukh Khan, often incorporates traditional dance steps into village celebrations, and they blend regional pride with popular culture.
travel_persona: Sunita Poli, who enjoys weekend getaways to Patnitop and Gulmarg’s snowy slopes, plans each trip with meticulous budgeting and they dream of someday exploring the historic streets of Varanasi and the coastal charm of Goa.
culinary_persona: Sunita Poli, an enthusiastic home cook, excels at preparing Dogri specialties such as madra, kalari and patta ka saag, loves using mustard seeds, walnuts and fresh herbs from her kitchen garden, and they often experiment with fragrant biryani during festive occasions.
persona: Sunita Poli blends disciplined household management with creative weaving, balances frugal saving with occasional fabric splurges, and they find calm in solitary hill walks.
cultural_background: Sunita hails from a Dogri‑speaking Hindu family in the rural outskirts of Jammu, Jammu & Kashmir. Her cultural upbringing is rooted in the traditions of the Dogra community, where festivals such as Lohri, Baisakhi and Navratri are celebrated with family gatherings, traditional music and folk dances. Growing up in a close‑knit village, she values community solidarity, respect for elders, and the practical customs of agrarian life, such as seasonal farming cycles and home‑based crafts. The rural setting and her family's Hindu rituals have instilled a sense of duty, modesty and reverence for nature.
linguistic_background: Sunita’s mother tongue is Dogri, which she speaks fluently and reads and writes comfortably in the Devanagari script used for Dogri. She acquired Hindi as a second language through school; she can converse confidently, understand everyday media, and read simple texts, though her writing is functional rather than sophisticated. Her formal education up to the Matric level gave her basic literacy in Hindi and limited exposure to English, allowing her to comprehend common signs and instructions but not to use English for detailed communication.
skills_and_expertise: Sunita excels in household management, including budgeting, meal planning and maintaining a clean, organized home. She is proficient in preparing traditional Dogri dishes as well as other Indian cuisines. Her caregiving skills cover child‑rearing and basic first‑aid. She also has practical knowledge of gardening and home‑based crafts such as weaving and embroidery. Additionally, she coordinates small community events and possesses basic computer skills for managing household accounts.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Household management", "Budgeting", "Cooking (traditional Dogri cuisine)", "Childcare", "Basic first aid", "Gardening", "Weaving and embroidery", "Community event coordination", "Basic computer literacy"]
hobbies_and_interests: Sunita enjoys cooking traditional Dogri dishes, tending to her kitchen garden, knitting and weaving simple garments, listening to local folk music, reading religious stories and scriptures, participating in village festivals, taking walks in the nearby hills, and volunteering for community clean‑up drives.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Cooking traditional Dogri dishes", "Gardening", "Knitting and weaving", "Listening to folk music", "Reading religious literature", "Participating in local festivals", "Walking in the hills", "Community volunteering"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Sunita aims to deepen her expertise in home management and gradually establish a small home‑based business producing hand‑woven shawls and embroidered home décor, leveraging the traditional crafts of Jammu. She plans to enroll in a short diploma program for early childhood care to enhance her caregiving skills and possibly start a modest daycare for neighbourhood children. Over the next few years, she aspires to achieve financial independence for her family, become a trusted community organizer for cultural events, and continue her personal growth through lifelong learning.
persona_clinical_summary: A 27-year-old female currently married, retired homemaker from a rural Dogri-speaking Hindu family in Jammu & Kashmir, with a matriculation education. She manages household finances, engages in traditional hand-weaving, and walks in local hills for exercise. Her lifestyle includes occasional fabric purchases and a diet featuring regional specialties like madra and patta ka saag.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.

- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

generated_text:
रेफरल [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-0815-001]] [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Sher-i-Kashmir Institute of Medical Sciences]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]] थमां
विषय: [[PATIENT_NAME|Rashmi Kumari]], [[AGE|27]] / [[GENDER|Female]], जिला [[DISTRICT|Jammu]]

कारण: पिछले त्रै महीने थमां सज्जे पासे दी कोख च पीर कन्नै पेशाब च खून (हेमट्यूरिया) ते बिना कुसै कारण दे वजन घट्ट होना, जिसकरी ऑन्कोलॉजिकल जांच ते इमेजिंग दी जरूरत ऐ।

मरीज 27 साल दी महिला गृहिणी ऐ, जेह्‌ड़ी जम्मू जिले, जम्मू ते कश्मीर दे ग्रामीण इलाके दी रहने आह्‌ली ऐ। उ’नें मुख्य शिकायत दे तौर पर कोख दे सज्जे पासे च भारी ते लगातार पीर दी शिकायत कीती ऐ, जेह्‌ड़ी जांघें बक्खी जांदी ऐ। उ’नें दे रूटीन यूरिन टैस्ट च पेशाब च सूक्ष्म रूप कन्नै खून (माइक्रोस्कोपिक हेमट्यूरिया) पाया गेआ। पीर हलचल कन्नै बधी जांदी ऐ ते पिछले त्रै महीने थमां धीरे-धीरे बधी रही ऐ। उ’नें दस्सेआ जे उ’नें दा वजन 6 किलोग्राम घट्ट होई गेआ ऐ, हालांकि उ’नें दी खान-पान दी आदतें च कोई बदलाव नेईं आया ऐ, जिसच क्षेत्रीय पकवान जि’यां मदरा ते पत्ता का साग शामल न। उ’नें गी न तां बुखार आया ऐ, न कम्मर पीर ते न गै रात गी पसीना आंदा ऐ। मरीज ने गुर्दे दी पथरी, यूरिनरी ट्रैक्ट इन्फेक्शन या चोट दा कोई इतिहास नेईं दस्सेआ।

जांच दे दौरान, मरीज सचेत ते जागरूक ऐ, उ’नें दा बॉडी मास इंडेक्स 21.5 ऐ। पेट दी जांच च सज्जे कोस्टोवर्टेब्रल एंगल च पीर पाई गई। कोई बी स्पष्ट गांठ महसूस नेईं होई। वाइटल साइन्स स्थिर न। प्रयोगशाला जांच च हीमोग्लोबिन 12.1 g/dL, क्रिएटिनिन 1.1 mg/dL ते यूरिनैलिसीस च 3+ खून पाया गेआ ऐ, पर कोई कास्ट नेईं ऐ। साढ़े केंद्र च कीते गे दे पेट ते पेल्विस दे नॉन-कंट्रास्ट सी टी स्कैन च सज्जे गुर्दे दे ऊपरी हिस्से च 4.5 सेंटीमीटर दा मास (गांठ) ते हाइड्रोनेफ्रोसिस पाया गेआ ऐ। दूर दे मेटास्टेसिस दा कोई सबूत नेईं दिखदा।

इमेजिंग दे नतीजे गुर्दे दे मास दा संकेत दिंदे न, इसकरी मरीज गी अग्गें दी जांच लेई ऑन्कोलॉजी विभाग च रेफर कीता जांदा ऐ, जिसच बायोप्सी राहीं पक्का निदान ते स्टेजिंग वर्कअप शामल ऐ। मरीज दा [[MRN|MRN-JK-2024-0815-001]] तुंदी जानकारी लेई संलग्न ऐ। मरीज दा [[PHONE_NUMBER|9419876543]] फॉलो-अप लेई उपलब्ध ऐ। मरीज दे पिओ, [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Singh]], उ’नें दे कन्नै सलाह-मशवरे लेई आऊंगे। पछाण दे मकसद लेई, मरीज दा [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|J9876543]] ते [[DRIVING_LICENCE|JK01 20200012345]] दित्ता गेआ ऐ। अस तुंदी माहिर राय ते इलाज दी योजना दी दरख्वास्त करदे आं।
generated_text_en:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-0815-001]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Sher-i-Kashmir Institute of Medical Sciences]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|Rashmi Kumari]], [[AGE|27]] / [[GENDER|Female]], District [[DISTRICT|Jammu]]
Reason: Persistent right-sided flank pain with hematuria and unexplained weight loss over the past three months, requiring oncological evaluation and imaging.

Patient is a 27-year-old female homemaker from a rural area in Jammu district, Jammu & Kashmir. She presents with a chief complaint of dull, constant right flank pain radiating to the groin, associated with microscopic hematuria noted on a routine urine test. The pain is exacerbated by movement and has progressively worsened over the last three months. She reports a 6 kg weight loss despite maintaining her usual dietary habits, which include regional dishes like madra and patta ka saag. There is no history of fever, chills, or night sweats. The patient denies any history of kidney stones, urinary tract infections, or trauma.

On examination, the patient is alert and oriented, with a body mass index of 21.5. Abdominal examination reveals tenderness in the right costovertebral angle. No palpable masses are detected. Vital signs are stable. Laboratory investigations show a hemoglobin of 12.1 g/dL, a creatinine of 1.1 mg/dL, and a urinalysis confirming 3+ blood with no casts. A non-contrast CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis, performed at our facility, reveals a 4.5 cm mass in the upper pole of the right kidney with associated hydronephrosis. No evidence of distant metastasis is seen.

Given the imaging findings suggestive of a renal mass, the patient is referred to the Department of Oncology for further management, including a definitive diagnosis via biopsy and staging workup. The patient's [[MRN|MRN-JK-2024-0815-001]] is attached for your reference. The patient's [[PHONE_NUMBER|9419876543]] is available for follow-up. The patient's father, [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Singh]], will accompany her for the consultation. For identification purposes, the patient's [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|J9876543]] and [[DRIVING_LICENCE|JK01 20200012345]] are provided. We kindly request your expert opinion and management plan.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Radiology / Imaging Report for clinical domain Maternal Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Dogri (code=doi, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Maternal Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Jammu & Kashmir, district=Jammu.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Dogri.

PLACEMENT: Include [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] as the reporting radiologist (e.g. Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]]) — do not leave the doctor name as untagged prose.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 27
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Matric/Secondary
- zone: Rural
- state: Jammu & Kashmir
- district: Jammu
- first_language: Dogri
- clinical_persona_summary: A 27-year-old female currently married, retired homemaker from a Dogri-speaking Hindu family in rural Jammu, Jammu & Kashmir, with a matriculation education. She manages household finances, engages in hand-weaving, and enjoys solitary hill walks for stress relief. Her lifestyle includes occasional fabric purchases and a preference for traditional Dogri cuisine.

Document format guide for Radiology / Imaging Report (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Imaging report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] | [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|45]] [[GENDER|Female]]
MRN [[MRN|...]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|...]] | Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Findings: <radiology prose, untagged>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- ENCOUNTER_ID — Encounter / Visit ID e.g. [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text:
रेडियोलॉजी रिपोर्ट — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Jammu]] | [[PATIENT_NAME|Sunita Kumari]] [[AGE|27]] [[GENDER|Female]]
MRN [[MRN|MRN-JK-2024-0012]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-JK-2024-0891]] | रिपोर्ट डॉ [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Sharma]] द्वारा दी गई
निष्कर्ष: पेट दा अल्ट्रासाउंड परीक्षण मरीज उप्पर कीता गेआ। गर्भाशय (uterus) गर्भावधि उम्र लेई सामान्य आकार दा ऐ, जेह्‌ड़ा लगभग 18 हफ्तें दा ऐ। इक जीवित भ्रूण (fetus) सिर-पैह्‌लें (cephalic presentation) स्थिति च ऐ, जेह्‌दी बायोमेट्री ते एमनियोटिक फ्लूइड इंडेक्स सामान्य ऐ। प्लेसेंटा (placenta) अग्गें बक्खी ऐ ते ग्रेड 2 परिपक्वता दा ऐ। गर्भाशय च कोई खास घाऽ या एडनेक्सल मास (adnexal masses) नेईं लब्भे। डॉपलर फ्लो स्टडीज सामान्य गर्भाशय ते गर्भनाल धमनी (umbilical artery) दी तरंगें गी दस्सदे न। जिगर (liver), पित्ताशय (gallbladder), गुर्दे (kidneys), ते तिल्ली (spleen) सामान्य लब्भे न। कोई मुक्त तरल (free fluid) या जलोदर (ascites) नेईं दिक्खेआ गेआ।

रिपोर्ट 15 अगस्त, 2024 गी पूरी कीती गई। मरीज दा [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]] इस अध्ययन कन्नै जुड़े दा ऐ। कुसै बी पुच्छ-गिच्छ लेई, अस्पताल कन्नै [[PHONE_NUMBER|9419012345]] उप्पर संपर्क करो। [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-JK-001]] रेडियोलॉजी विभाग रिपोर्ट दी पुष्टि करदा ऐ। मरीज [[VILLAGE|Nagrota]] च [[DISTRICT|Jammu]] दे अंदर रौह्‌ंदी ऐ। ओह्‌दा [[ABHA_ADDRESS|sunita.kumari@abdm]] राष्ट्रीय डिजिटल स्वास्थ्य मिशन कन्नै पंजीकृत ऐ।

Radiology report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Jammu]] | [[PATIENT_NAME|Sunita Kumari]] [[AGE|27]] [[GENDER|Female]]
MRN [[MRN|MRN-JK-2024-0012]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-JK-2024-0891]] | Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Sharma]]
Findings: Ultrasound examination of the abdomen was performed on the patient. The uterus is of normal size for gestational age, measuring approximately 18 weeks. A single live fetus is present in cephalic presentation with normal biometry and amniotic fluid index. Placenta is anterior and grade 2 maturity. No focal uterine lesions or adnexal masses identified. Doppler flow studies show normal uterine and umbilical artery waveforms. The liver, gallbladder, kidneys, and spleen appear unremarkable. No free fluid or ascites noted.

Report completed on the 15th of August, 2024. The patient's [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]] is linked to this study. For any queries, contact the hospital at [[PHONE_NUMBER|9419012345]]. The [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-JK-001]] radiology department confirms the report. The patient resides in [[VILLAGE|Nagrota]] within [[DISTRICT|Jammu]]. Her [[ABHA_ADDRESS|sunita.kumari@abdm]] is registered with the National Digital Health Mission.

professional_persona: Sunita Poli, a home‑based craft entrepreneur and aspiring early‑childhood caregiver, combines her expertise in household budgeting, hand‑weaving, and community event coordination, and they plan to launch a line of hand‑woven shawls while preparing to open a modest neighbourhood daycare.
linguistic_persona: Sunita Poli, fluent in Dogri and comfortable conversing in Hindi, reads and writes Dogri in Devanagari, understands everyday Hindi media, and they navigate basic English signs, making their multilingual communication rooted in regional languages.
sports_persona: Sunita Poli, an avid hill‑walker and occasional cricket fan, follows Real Kashmir FC’s matches and joins a local women’s walking group that meets twice a week for fitness and fresh air, and they find the routine grounding.
arts_persona: Sunita Poli, an avid listener of Dogri folk songs by Ravindra Singh and a fan of Bollywood classics starring Shah Rukh Khan, often incorporates traditional dance steps into village celebrations, and they blend regional pride with popular culture.
travel_persona: Sunita Poli, who enjoys weekend getaways to Patnitop and Gulmarg’s snowy slopes, plans each trip with meticulous budgeting and they dream of someday exploring the historic streets of Varanasi and the coastal charm of Goa.
culinary_persona: Sunita Poli, an enthusiastic home cook, excels at preparing Dogri specialties such as madra, kalari and patta ka saag, loves using mustard seeds, walnuts and fresh herbs from her kitchen garden, and they often experiment with fragrant biryani during festive occasions.
persona: Sunita Poli blends disciplined household management with creative weaving, balances frugal saving with occasional fabric splurges, and they find calm in solitary hill walks.
cultural_background: Sunita hails from a Dogri‑speaking Hindu family in the rural outskirts of Jammu, Jammu & Kashmir. Her cultural upbringing is rooted in the traditions of the Dogra community, where festivals such as Lohri, Baisakhi and Navratri are celebrated with family gatherings, traditional music and folk dances. Growing up in a close‑knit village, she values community solidarity, respect for elders, and the practical customs of agrarian life, such as seasonal farming cycles and home‑based crafts. The rural setting and her family's Hindu rituals have instilled a sense of duty, modesty and reverence for nature.
linguistic_background: Sunita’s mother tongue is Dogri, which she speaks fluently and reads and writes comfortably in the Devanagari script used for Dogri. She acquired Hindi as a second language through school; she can converse confidently, understand everyday media, and read simple texts, though her writing is functional rather than sophisticated. Her formal education up to the Matric level gave her basic literacy in Hindi and limited exposure to English, allowing her to comprehend common signs and instructions but not to use English for detailed communication.
skills_and_expertise: Sunita excels in household management, including budgeting, meal planning and maintaining a clean, organized home. She is proficient in preparing traditional Dogri dishes as well as other Indian cuisines. Her caregiving skills cover child‑rearing and basic first‑aid. She also has practical knowledge of gardening and home‑based crafts such as weaving and embroidery. Additionally, she coordinates small community events and possesses basic computer skills for managing household accounts.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Household management", "Budgeting", "Cooking (traditional Dogri cuisine)", "Childcare", "Basic first aid", "Gardening", "Weaving and embroidery", "Community event coordination", "Basic computer literacy"]
hobbies_and_interests: Sunita enjoys cooking traditional Dogri dishes, tending to her kitchen garden, knitting and weaving simple garments, listening to local folk music, reading religious stories and scriptures, participating in village festivals, taking walks in the nearby hills, and volunteering for community clean‑up drives.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Cooking traditional Dogri dishes", "Gardening", "Knitting and weaving", "Listening to folk music", "Reading religious literature", "Participating in local festivals", "Walking in the hills", "Community volunteering"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Sunita aims to deepen her expertise in home management and gradually establish a small home‑based business producing hand‑woven shawls and embroidered home décor, leveraging the traditional crafts of Jammu. She plans to enroll in a short diploma program for early childhood care to enhance her caregiving skills and possibly start a modest daycare for neighbourhood children. Over the next few years, she aspires to achieve financial independence for her family, become a trusted community organizer for cultural events, and continue her personal growth through lifelong learning.
persona_clinical_summary: A 27-year-old female from Jammu & Kashmir, Dogri-speaking, currently married and retired, with a secondary education. Lives in a rural zone and manages household responsibilities. Clinically relevant lifestyle includes occasional fabric spending and solitary hill walks for stress relief.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete ER Triage & Nursing Notes for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Dogri (code=doi, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Jammu & Kashmir, district=Jammu.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Dogri.

PLACEMENT (vehicle): Put VEHICLE_REGISTRATION exactly once in the arrival/RTA/ambulance line. Do not dump or repeat it.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 27
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Matric/Secondary
- zone: Rural
- state: Jammu & Kashmir
- district: Jammu
- first_language: Dogri
- clinical_persona_summary: A 27-year-old female from Jammu & Kashmir, Dogri-speaking, currently married and retired, with a secondary education. Lives in a rural zone and manages household responsibilities. Clinically relevant lifestyle includes occasional fabric spending and solitary hill walks for stress relief.

Document format guide for ER Triage & Nursing Notes (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
ER triage — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|22]] [[GENDER|Male]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|...]]
Arrived by ambulance / RTA vehicle [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|PB10AB1234]] (exactly once).
Relative [[RELATIVE_NAME|...]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|...]] Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Vitals / acuity: <short notes>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ENCOUNTER_ID — Encounter / Visit ID e.g. [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION — Vehicle Registration Number e.g. [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

[[PATIENT_NAME|Sharda Devi]] [[AGE|27]] [[GENDER|Female]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|04]]

Relative [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Kumar]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9419876543]] Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Gupta]]

Vitals / acuity: मरीज़ होश च ऐ, alert ऐ, ते x3 oriented ऐ। Vitals stable न। T 98.4F, BP 120/80, HR 88, RR 18, SpO2 99% room air पर। Pain score 4/10।

Nursing Notes:
मरीज़ ने अचानक तेज़, सज्जे पासे छाती च पीड़ दी रिपोर्ट दित्ती ऐ जेह्ड़ी कंधे तक्कर जा करदी ऐ, लगभग 2 घंटे पैह्‌लें शुरू होई ही जिसलै ओह्‌ इक कल्ले पहाड़ी सैर पर हा। साह्‌ फुल्लने, दिल दी धड़कन तेज़ होने, या पसीने आने शा इनकार करदा ऐ। अजेही घटना दा कोई इतिहास नेईं ऐ। आखरी खाना 4 घंटे पैह्‌लें हा। कोई बी allergies नेईं पता। कोई बी current medications नेईं न। मरीज़ इक homemaker ऐ, Jammu district दे ग्रामीण इलाके च रौह्‌न्दा ऐ। Repeat check पर vitals stable न। Oxygen saturation 99% पर बनी दी ऐ। मरीज़ गी bed rest पर रौह्‌ने ते monitoring लेई सलाह दित्ती गेई ऐ। IV access खब्बे हत्थ च 18G cannula कन्नै बनाया गेआ ऐ। CBC, CMP, ते troponin levels लेई खून दे samples भेजे गे न। Chest X-ray order कीता गेआ ऐ। पीड़ गी paracetamol 500mg orally कन्नै manage कीता गेआ ऐ। मरीज़ गी उनें symptoms बारै दस्सेआ गेआ ऐ जि’नें दी तुरंत report करनी चाहिदी ऐ।

[[PATIENT_NAME|Sharda Devi]] [[AGE|27]] [[GENDER|Female]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|04]]
