Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

[[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]: appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] at [[HOSPITAL_NAME|PHC Kanpur]] on 21-May 10:30 with [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sharma]]. Confirm on [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]. MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-001]].

MANDATORY inline entities (6 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

professional_persona: Deshmukh Kalakumbe, a seasoned rural household manager skilled in regional cuisine, budgeting, and minor home repairs, is channeling their practical curiosity into a modest family‑run domestic‑service training venture aimed at steady retirement income.
linguistic_persona: Deshmukh Kalakumbe comfortably converses in Marathi, handles Hindi with functional proficiency despite occasional slips, and uses basic English solely for simple instructions and signs, reflecting their pragmatic multilingual skill set rooted in village life.
sports_persona: Deshmukh Kalakumbe follows cricket passionately on the village radio, cheering for the Maharashtra Ranji team and the IPL side Royal Challengers Bangalore, and they occasionally join friendly matches at the village ground with the Kolhapur Cricket Club.
arts_persona: Deshmukh Kalakumbe spends evenings humming devotional bhajans and Marathi folk tunes, especially favorites by Shahir Sable and the contemporary duo Ajay‑Atul, and they also participate in village Lavni performances during festivals.
travel_persona: Deshmukh Kalakumbe enjoys short weekend getaways to Lonavala’s misty hills and Mahabaleshwar’s strawberry farms, occasionally dreaming of a longer visit to Goa’s beaches, yet plans trips conservatively to fit their modest budget and family duties.
culinary_persona: Deshmukh Kalakumbe delights in preparing bhakri with fresh millet flour, puran poli stuffed with jaggery and lentils, and varan bhaat, often spicing them with home‑grown chilies, coriander, and a dash of hing; they favor mango‑pickle as a tangy accompaniment and relish tasting the seasonal mangoes of their orchard.
persona: Deshmukh Kalakumbe, a 51‑year‑old rural household maestro, fuses practical thrift with a curious flair for new ideas, runs a budding domestic‑service venture, chants bhajans, tracks cricket scores on the radio, and occasionally treats themselves to a shiny new radio despite a disciplined budget.
cultural_background: Deshmukh Kalakumbe belongs to a Marathi-speaking farming community in the rural outskirts of Kolhapur, Maharashtra. His family name 'Deshmukh' historically denoted a village headman, and the sense of community leadership still runs in his veins. He grew up surrounded by sugarcane fields, mango orchards, and the rhythms of monsoon life. Traditional Maharashtrian customs shape his daily routine – the family gathers for a modest early‑morning meal of bhakri and zunka, festivals such as Ganesh Chaturthi, Diwali and Holi are celebrated with enthusiasm, and local folk arts like Tamasha and dhol‑tasha music are part of his cultural fabric. The rural setting has nurtured a practical mindset coupled with a curiosity about new ideas, which he balances with respect for age‑old practices. His cultural worldview reflects a blend of agrarian values, joint‑family solidarity, and a modest pride in his regional identity.
linguistic_background: Deshmukh Kalakumbe grew up speaking Marathi at home and in the village, and he has full literacy in Marathi – he can read and write the Devanagari script with ease. Hindi, taught in school, is his second language; he can converse comfortably, understands newspapers and can read simple Hindi texts, though occasional grammatical slips occur. His middle‑school education gave him basic English exposure, enough to follow simple instructions and comprehend everyday signs, but he is not fluent in speaking or writing English. Overall, his linguistic repertoire is anchored in Marathi, supplemented by functional Hindi, and a modest practical command of English for basic tasks.
skills_and_expertise: Deshmukh is adept at managing all aspects of household operations in a rural setting. His expertise includes preparing authentic Maharashtrian meals such as bhakri, puran poli, and varan bhaat; maintaining cleanliness and organizing living spaces; handling laundry with traditional washing methods; budgeting household expenses and managing cash flow; performing basic repairs like fixing leaky taps, minor carpentry, and simple electrical tasks; tending to kitchen and vegetable gardens, ensuring year‑round produce; providing caregiving support for children and elderly family members; coordinating festival preparations, including decorations, puja arrangements, and community feasts; and training junior domestic helpers in practical skills using his pragmatic yet curious approach.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Cooking authentic Maharashtrian dishes (e.g., bhakri, puran poli, varan bhaat)', 'Household cleaning and organization', 'Laundry using traditional washing methods', 'Household budgeting and cash‑flow management', 'Basic home repairs (plumbing, carpentry, simple electrical work)', 'Kitchen and vegetable garden maintenance', 'Childcare and elderly assistance', 'Festival preparation and coordination (decorations, puja, community feasts)', 'Training junior domestic helpers in practical skills', 'Problem‑solving with a pragmatic and curious approach (jugaad)']
hobbies_and_interests: Deshmukh enjoys listening to devotional bhajans and Marathi folk songs, often humming tunes while cooking. He is an avid cricket fan, following domestic tournaments and the Indian team's matches on the radio. In his spare time, he cultivates a small kitchen garden, growing tomatoes, chilies and okra, and experiments with new Maharashtrian recipes. He likes reading simple Marathi novels and short stories, especially those that reflect rural life. Community festivals attract his participation, where he helps with decorations and traditional dances. Occasionally, he attends basic computer classes at the local community centre to learn simple spreadsheet skills for household budgeting. He also likes taking leisurely walks in the fields surrounding his village, appreciating nature and reflecting on daily tasks.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to devotional bhajans and Marathi folk songs', 'Following cricket matches and tournaments', 'Cultivating a kitchen garden with vegetables', 'Experimenting with new Maharashtrian recipes', 'Reading Marathi novels and short stories about rural life', 'Participating in community festivals (decorations, traditional dances)', 'Attending basic computer classes for budgeting', 'Taking leisurely walks in village fields']
career_goals_and_ambitions: At 51, Deshmukh aims to transition from sole household work to a modest family‑run domestic‑service venture that can generate steady income for his retirement and provide employment opportunities for younger relatives. He intends to formalise his expertise by offering training workshops on practical housekeeping, cooking, and basic home‑maintenance for aspiring helpers in his village. Parallelly, he dreams of supporting his children's aspirations—ensuring his son can pursue higher education in a technical field and his daughter can start a small tailoring business. He also wishes to invest in a small piece of land for vegetable cultivation, using it both as an extra source of income and as a means to maintain his connection to the agrarian lifestyle he cherishes.
persona_clinical_summary: A 51-year-old male from Kolhapur, Maharashtra, currently married, with middle education, working as a domestic housekeeper. He is Marathi-speaking, resides in a rural area, and manages household finances while pursuing a small-scale domestic service training venture. His lifestyle includes listening to cricket commentary on the radio, practicing devotional bhajans, and preparing traditional Maharashtrian cuisine.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]

  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete PHC Register Entry (Primary Health Centre) for clinical domain Surgical.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Marathi (code=mr, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Maharashtra, district=Kolhapur.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Marathi.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 51
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: House Keeper (Domestic)
- education_level: Middle
- zone: Rural
- state: Maharashtra
- district: Kolhapur
- first_language: Marathi
- clinical_persona_summary: A 51-year-old male from Kolhapur, Maharashtra, currently married, with middle education, working as a domestic housekeeper. He is Marathi-speaking, resides in a rural area, and manages household finances while pursuing a small-scale domestic service training venture. His lifestyle includes listening to cricket commentary on the radio, practicing devotional bhajans, and preparing traditional Maharashtrian cuisine.

Document format guide for PHC Register Entry (Primary Health Centre) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
PHC register — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|PHC ...]] Village [[VILLAGE|...]] District [[DISTRICT|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|19]] [[GENDER|Female]] | brief entry line

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- VILLAGE — Village e.g. [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- ASHA_WORKER_NAME — ASHA Worker Name e.g. [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
- BPL_RATION_CARD — BPL / Ration Card Number e.g. [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
- VOTER_ID — Voter ID e.g. [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text:
प्राथमिक आरोग्य केंद्र नोंदणी — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Primary Health Centre, Shirol]] गाव [[VILLAGE|Shirol]] जिल्हा [[DISTRICT|Kolhapur]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Ramesh Patil]] [[AGE|51]] [[GENDER|Male]] | रुग्णाने उजव्या बाजूच्या पोटाच्या खालच्या भागात तीव्र वेदना, ताप आणि उलट्यांसह हजेरी लावली.

मुख्य तक्रार:
रुग्णाने अचानक उजव्या बाजूच्या पोटाच्या खालच्या भागात तीव्र वेदना सुरू झाल्याचे सांगितले आहे, ज्यासोबत ताप आणि दोनदा उलट्या झाल्या आहेत. वेदना सुमारे 24 तास आधी सुरू झाल्या असून त्या हळूहळू वाढत आहेत.

सध्याच्या आजाराचा इतिहास:
श्री. रमेश पाटील, वय 51 वर्षे, पुरुष, शिराळ गाव, कोल्हापूर जिल्हा येथील रहिवासी, यांना उजव्या बाजूच्या पोटाच्या खालच्या भागात तीव्र वेदनांचा 24 तासांचा इतिहास आहे. वेदना तीव्र, सतत आणि तीव्रतेमध्ये 8/10 इतक्या आहेत. त्यांना ताप (घरी मोजलेला 101°F (38.3°C)) आणि दोनदा उलट्या झाल्याचे त्यांनी सांगितले. त्यांनी शौचाच्या सवयीत कोणताही बदल, रक्तासह शौच (hematochezia) किंवा काळ्या रंगाचे शौच (melena) झाल्याचे नाकारले आहे. त्यांना यापूर्वी अशा प्रकारच्या घटनांचा कोणताही इतिहास नाही. त्यांना भूक कमी लागणे आणि सर्वसाधारण अशक्तपणा जाणवत आहे.

पूर्वीचा वैद्यकीय इतिहास:
- उच्च रक्तदाब (5 वर्षांपूर्वी निदान झालेला, औषधांवर आहे)
- टाईप 2 मधुमेह (3 वर्षांपूर्वी निदान झालेला, तोंडावाटे घ्यावयाच्या हायपोग्लायसेमिक औषधांवर आहे)
- कोणतीही ज्ञात शस्त्रक्रिया किंवा रुग्णालयात दाखल झाल्याचा इतिहास नाही.

औषधे:
- एमलोडिपिन 5mg दिवसातून एकदा
- मेटफॉर्मिन 500mg दिवसातून दोनदा
- ताप असल्यास गरजेनुसार पॅरासिटामॉल 500mg

ॲलर्जी:
- कोणतीही ज्ञात औषध ॲलर्जी नाही

कौटुंबिक इतिहास:
- वडील: मृत, त्यांना उच्च रक्तदाब आणि मधुमेह होता
- आई: जिवंत, उच्च रक्तदाब असलेली
- पत्नी: जिवंत, कोणतीही ज्ञात जुनाट आजार नाही

सामाजिक इतिहास:
- विवाहित, पत्नी आणि दोन मुलांसोबत राहतात
- व्यवसाय: घरकाम करणारा (घरगुती)
- धूम्रपान न करणारा, अधूनमधून तंबाखू चर्वण करतो
- मद्यपान: सामाजिक मद्यपान करणारा, आठवड्यातून 2-3 युनिट्स

तपासणी:
- सामान्य: शुद्धीवर, सुज्ञ, आजारी वाटत आहे, ताप आहे
- पोट: मऊ, फुगलेले, उजव्या बाजूच्या खालच्या भागात संरक्षक स्नायूंच्या ताणासह (guarding) आणि रिबाउंड टेंडरनेससह वेदना जाणवत आहेत. आतड्यांचे आवाज कमी झाले आहेत. कोणतेही गाठ जाणवत नाही.
- मलाशय: कोणतीही वेदना नाही, कोणतीही गाठ नाही.
- जीवनचिन्हे: तापमान 101.2°F (38.4°C), नाडी 108/मिनिट, रक्तदाब 140/90 mmHg, श्वसन दर 20/मिनिट, खोलीच्या हवेत SpO2 98%.

तपासण्या:
- पूर्ण रक्त तपासणी: न्यूट्रोफिलियासह ल्युकोसाइटोसिस (WBC 14,500/µL)
- सीरम क्रिएटिनिन: 1.1 mg/dL
- सीरम इलेक्ट्रोलाइट्स: सामान्य मर्यादेत
- लघवीची तपासणी: कोणतीही लक्षणीय असामान्यता नाही
- पोटाचा क्ष-किरण (Abdominal X-ray): नॉन-स्पेसिफिक इलियस पॅटर्न, कोणतीही मोकळी हवा नाही.

मूल्यमापन:
1. तीव्र अपेंडिसायटिस, संशयित
2. संसर्गामुळे होणारा ताप
3. उच्च रक्तदाब, नियंत्रणात
4. टाईप 2 मधुमेह, नियंत्रणात

योजना:
1. शस्त्रक्रिया मूल्यांकनासाठी रुग्णाला दाखल करा.
2. आयव्ही फ्लुइड्स (नॉर्मल सलाईन 100 mL/तास) सुरू करा.
3. ब्रॉड-स्पेक्ट्रम आयव्ही अँटीबायोटिक्स (सेफ्ट्रिअॅक्सोन 1g आयव्ही दर 24 तासांनी आणि मेट्रोनिडाझोल 500mg आयव्ही दर 8 तासांनी) सुरू करा.
4. आयव्ही पॅरासिटामॉल 1g दर 6 तासांनी देऊन वेदनाशमन करा.
5. एनपीओ (तोंडवाटे काहीही न देणे).
6. तातडीने शस्त्रक्रिया सल्लामसलत मागितली आहे.
7. सर्जनच्या सल्ल्यानुसार शस्त्रक्रियेची व्यवस्था करा.

generated_text_en:
PHC register — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Primary Health Centre, Shirol]] Village [[VILLAGE|Shirol]] District [[DISTRICT|Kolhapur]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Ramesh Patil]] [[AGE|51]] [[GENDER|Male]] | Patient presented with acute right lower quadrant abdominal pain, fever, and vomiting.

Chief Complaint:
Patient reports sudden onset of severe abdominal pain localized to the right lower quadrant, associated with fever and two episodes of non-bilious vomiting. Pain started approximately 24 hours ago and is progressively worsening.

History of Present Illness:
Mr. Ramesh Patil, a 51-year-old male domestic housekeeper from Shirol village, Kolhapur district, presents with a 24-hour history of acute right lower quadrant abdominal pain. The pain is sharp, constant, and rated 8/10 in severity. He reports associated fever, measured at home as 101°F (38.3°C), and two episodes of vomiting. He denies any change in bowel habits, hematochezia, or melena. He has no history of similar episodes. He reports decreased appetite and generalized weakness.

Past Medical History:
- Hypertension (diagnosed 5 years ago, on medication)
- Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (diagnosed 3 years ago, on oral hypoglycemics)
- No known surgeries or hospitalizations.

Medications:
- Amlodipine 5mg once daily
- Metformin 500mg twice daily
- Paracetamol 500mg as needed for fever

Allergies:
- No known drug allergies

Family History:
- Father: Deceased, had hypertension and diabetes
- Mother: Alive, hypertensive
- Wife: Alive, no known chronic illnesses

Social History:
- Married, lives with wife and two children
- Occupation: House Keeper (Domestic)
- Non-smoker, occasional tobacco chewing
- Alcohol: Social drinker, 2-3 units per week

Examination:
- General: Conscious, oriented, appears unwell, febrile
- Abdomen: Soft, distended, tender in right lower quadrant with guarding and rebound tenderness. Bowel sounds are diminished. No masses palpable.
- Rectal: No tenderness, no masses.
- Vital Signs: Temperature 101.2°F (38.4°C), Pulse 108/min, BP 140/90 mmHg, RR 20/min, SpO2 98% on room air.

Investigations:
- Complete Blood Count: Leukocytosis with neutrophilia (WBC 14,500/µL)
- Serum Creatinine: 1.1 mg/dL
- Serum Electrolytes: Within normal limits
- Urine Routine: No significant abnormalities
- Abdominal X-ray: Non-specific ileus pattern, no free air.

Assessment:
1. Acute Appendicitis, suspected
2. Fever secondary to infection
3. Hypertension, controlled
4. Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, controlled

Plan:
1. Admit patient for surgical evaluation.
2. Start IV fluids (Normal Saline 100 mL/hr).
3. Start broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (Ceftriaxone 1g IV q24h and Metronidazole 500mg IV q8h).
4. Analgesia with IV Paracetamol 1g q6h.
5. Keep NPO (nothing by mouth).
6. Urgent surgical consultation requested.
7. Arrange for surgical procedure as per surgeon's advice.

professional_persona: Deshmukh Kalakumbe, a seasoned rural household manager skilled in regional cuisine, budgeting, and minor home repairs, is channeling their practical curiosity into a modest family‑run domestic‑service training venture aimed at steady retirement income.
linguistic_persona: Deshmukh Kalakumbe comfortably converses in Marathi, handles Hindi with functional proficiency despite occasional slips, and uses basic English solely for simple instructions and signs, reflecting their pragmatic multilingual skill set rooted in village life.
sports_persona: Deshmukh Kalakumbe follows cricket passionately on the village radio, cheering for the Maharashtra Ranji team and the IPL side Royal Challengers Bangalore, and they occasionally join friendly matches at the village ground with the Kolhapur Cricket Club.
arts_persona: Deshmukh Kalakumbe spends evenings humming devotional bhajans and Marathi folk tunes, especially favorites by Shahir Sable and the contemporary duo Ajay‑Atul, and they also participate in village Lavni performances during festivals.
travel_persona: Deshmukh Kalakumbe enjoys short weekend getaways to Lonavala’s misty hills and Mahabaleshwar’s strawberry farms, occasionally dreaming of a longer visit to Goa’s beaches, yet plans trips conservatively to fit their modest budget and family duties.
culinary_persona: Deshmukh Kalakumbe delights in preparing bhakri with fresh millet flour, puran poli stuffed with jaggery and lentils, and varan bhaat, often spicing them with home‑grown chilies, coriander, and a dash of hing; they favor mango‑pickle as a tangy accompaniment and relish tasting the seasonal mangoes of their orchard.
persona: Deshmukh Kalakumbe, a 51‑year‑old rural household maestro, fuses practical thrift with a curious flair for new ideas, runs a budding domestic‑service venture, chants bhajans, tracks cricket scores on the radio, and occasionally treats themselves to a shiny new radio despite a disciplined budget.
cultural_background: Deshmukh Kalakumbe belongs to a Marathi-speaking farming community in the rural outskirts of Kolhapur, Maharashtra. His family name 'Deshmukh' historically denoted a village headman, and the sense of community leadership still runs in his veins. He grew up surrounded by sugarcane fields, mango orchards, and the rhythms of monsoon life. Traditional Maharashtrian customs shape his daily routine – the family gathers for a modest early‑morning meal of bhakri and zunka, festivals such as Ganesh Chaturthi, Diwali and Holi are celebrated with enthusiasm, and local folk arts like Tamasha and dhol‑tasha music are part of his cultural fabric. The rural setting has nurtured a practical mindset coupled with a curiosity about new ideas, which he balances with respect for age‑old practices. His cultural worldview reflects a blend of agrarian values, joint‑family solidarity, and a modest pride in his regional identity.
linguistic_background: Deshmukh Kalakumbe grew up speaking Marathi at home and in the village, and he has full literacy in Marathi – he can read and write the Devanagari script with ease. Hindi, taught in school, is his second language; he can converse comfortably, understands newspapers and can read simple Hindi texts, though occasional grammatical slips occur. His middle‑school education gave him basic English exposure, enough to follow simple instructions and comprehend everyday signs, but he is not fluent in speaking or writing English. Overall, his linguistic repertoire is anchored in Marathi, supplemented by functional Hindi, and a modest practical command of English for basic tasks.
skills_and_expertise: Deshmukh is adept at managing all aspects of household operations in a rural setting. His expertise includes preparing authentic Maharashtrian meals such as bhakri, puran poli, and varan bhaat; maintaining cleanliness and organizing living spaces; handling laundry with traditional washing methods; budgeting household expenses and managing cash flow; performing basic repairs like fixing leaky taps, minor carpentry, and simple electrical tasks; tending to kitchen and vegetable gardens, ensuring year‑round produce; providing caregiving support for children and elderly family members; coordinating festival preparations, including decorations, puja arrangements, and community feasts; and training junior domestic helpers in practical skills using his pragmatic yet curious approach.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Cooking authentic Maharashtrian dishes (e.g., bhakri, puran poli, varan bhaat)', 'Household cleaning and organization', 'Laundry using traditional washing methods', 'Household budgeting and cash‑flow management', 'Basic home repairs (plumbing, carpentry, simple electrical work)', 'Kitchen and vegetable garden maintenance', 'Childcare and elderly assistance', 'Festival preparation and coordination (decorations, puja, community feasts)', 'Training junior domestic helpers in practical skills', 'Problem‑solving with a pragmatic and curious approach (jugaad)']
hobbies_and_interests: Deshmukh enjoys listening to devotional bhajans and Marathi folk songs, often humming tunes while cooking. He is an avid cricket fan, following domestic tournaments and the Indian team's matches on the radio. In his spare time, he cultivates a small kitchen garden, growing tomatoes, chilies and okra, and experiments with new Maharashtrian recipes. He likes reading simple Marathi novels and short stories, especially those that reflect rural life. Community festivals attract his participation, where he helps with decorations and traditional dances. Occasionally, he attends basic computer classes at the local community centre to learn simple spreadsheet skills for household budgeting. He also likes taking leisurely walks in the fields surrounding his village, appreciating nature and reflecting on daily tasks.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to devotional bhajans and Marathi folk songs', 'Following cricket matches and tournaments', 'Cultivating a kitchen garden with vegetables', 'Experimenting with new Maharashtrian recipes', 'Reading Marathi novels and short stories about rural life', 'Participating in community festivals (decorations, traditional dances)', 'Attending basic computer classes for budgeting', 'Taking leisurely walks in village fields']
career_goals_and_ambitions: At 51, Deshmukh aims to transition from sole household work to a modest family‑run domestic‑service venture that can generate steady income for his retirement and provide employment opportunities for younger relatives. He intends to formalise his expertise by offering training workshops on practical housekeeping, cooking, and basic home‑maintenance for aspiring helpers in his village. Parallelly, he dreams of supporting his children's aspirations—ensuring his son can pursue higher education in a technical field and his daughter can start a small tailoring business. He also wishes to invest in a small piece of land for vegetable cultivation, using it both as an extra source of income and as a means to maintain his connection to the agrarian lifestyle he cherishes.

He is Marathi-speaking, with a background in regional community leadership, and manages household budgeting and regional cuisine. His lifestyle includes listening to cricket commentary, practicing devotional bhajans, and occasional participation in village folk performances.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]

  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]

  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete ASHA Worker Note for clinical domain Oncology / Complex Chronic Care.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Marathi (code=mr, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Oncology / Complex Chronic Care — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Maharashtra, district=Kolhapur.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Marathi.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 51
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: House Keeper (Domestic)
- education_level: Middle
- zone: Rural
- state: Maharashtra
- district: Kolhapur
- first_language: Marathi
- clinical_persona_summary: A 51-year-old male from rural Kolhapur, Maharashtra, currently married, with middle education, who works as a domestic housekeeper. He is Marathi-speaking, with a background in regional community leadership, and manages household budgeting and regional cuisine. His lifestyle includes listening to cricket commentary, practicing devotional bhajans, and occasional participation in village folk performances.

Document format guide for ASHA Worker Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
ASHA note — Village [[VILLAGE|...]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Beneficiary [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|26]] / [[GENDER|Female]]
ASHA: [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|...]] | Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Visit findings: <2-4 short sentences>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]

professional_persona: Deshmukh Kalakumbe, a seasoned rural household manager skilled in regional cuisine, budgeting, and minor home repairs, is channeling their practical curiosity into a modest family‑run domestic‑service training venture aimed at steady retirement income.
linguistic_persona: Deshmukh Kalakumbe comfortably converses in Marathi, handles Hindi with functional proficiency despite occasional slips, and uses basic English solely for simple instructions and signs, reflecting their pragmatic multilingual skill set rooted in village life.
sports_persona: Deshmukh Kalakumbe follows cricket passionately on the village radio, cheering for the Maharashtra Ranji team and the IPL side Royal Challengers Bangalore, and they occasionally join friendly matches at the village ground with the Kolhapur Cricket Club.
arts_persona: Deshmukh Kalakumbe spends evenings humming devotional bhajans and Marathi folk tunes, especially favorites by Shahir Sable and the contemporary duo Ajay‑Atul, and they also participate in village Lavni performances during festivals.
travel_persona: Deshmukh Kalakumbe enjoys short weekend getaways to Lonavala’s misty hills and Mahabaleshwar’s strawberry farms, occasionally dreaming of a longer visit to Goa’s beaches, yet plans trips conservatively to fit their modest budget and family duties.
culinary_persona: Deshmukh Kalakumbe delights in preparing bhakri with fresh millet flour, puran poli stuffed with jaggery and lentils, and varan bhaat, often spicing them with home‑grown chilies, coriander, and a dash of hing; they favor mango‑pickle as a tangy accompaniment and relish tasting the seasonal mangoes of their orchard.
persona: Deshmukh Kalakumbe, a 51‑year‑old rural household maestro, fuses practical thrift with a curious flair for new ideas, runs a budding domestic‑service venture, chants bhajans, tracks cricket scores on the radio, and occasionally treats themselves to a shiny new radio despite a disciplined budget.
cultural_background: Deshmukh Kalakumbe belongs to a Marathi-speaking farming community in the rural outskirts of Kolhapur, Maharashtra. His family name 'Deshmukh' historically denoted a village headman, and the sense of community leadership still runs in his veins. He grew up surrounded by sugarcane fields, mango orchards, and the rhythms of monsoon life. Traditional Maharashtrian customs shape his daily routine – the family gathers for a modest early‑morning meal of bhakri and zunka, festivals such as Ganesh Chaturthi, Diwali and Holi are celebrated with enthusiasm, and local folk arts like Tamasha and dhol‑tasha music are part of his cultural fabric. The rural setting has nurtured a practical mindset coupled with a curiosity about new ideas, which he balances with respect for age‑old practices. His cultural worldview reflects a blend of agrarian values, joint‑family solidarity, and a modest pride in his regional identity.
linguistic_background: Deshmukh Kalakumbe grew up speaking Marathi at home and in the village, and he has full literacy in Marathi – he can read and write the Devanagari script with ease. Hindi, taught in school, is his second language; he can converse comfortably, understands newspapers and can read simple Hindi texts, though occasional grammatical slips occur. His middle‑school education gave him basic English exposure, enough to follow simple instructions and comprehend everyday signs, but he is not fluent in speaking or writing English. Overall, his linguistic repertoire is anchored in Marathi, supplemented by functional Hindi, and a modest practical command of English for basic tasks.
skills_and_expertise: Deshmukh is adept at managing all aspects of household operations in a rural setting. His expertise includes preparing authentic Maharashtrian meals such as bhakri, puran poli, and varan bhaat; maintaining cleanliness and organizing living spaces; handling laundry with traditional washing methods; budgeting household expenses and managing cash flow; performing basic repairs like fixing leaky taps, minor carpentry, and simple electrical tasks; tending to kitchen and vegetable gardens, ensuring year‑round produce; providing caregiving support for children and elderly family members; coordinating festival preparations, including decorations, puja arrangements, and community feasts; and training junior domestic helpers in practical skills using his pragmatic yet curious approach.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Cooking authentic Maharashtrian dishes (e.g., bhakri, puran poli, varan bhaat)', 'Household cleaning and organization', 'Laundry using traditional washing methods', 'Household budgeting and cash‑flow management', 'Basic home repairs (plumbing, carpentry, simple electrical work)', 'Kitchen and vegetable garden maintenance', 'Childcare and elderly assistance', 'Festival preparation and coordination (decorations, puja, community feasts)', 'Training junior domestic helpers in practical skills', 'Problem‑solving with a pragmatic and curious approach (jugaad)']
hobbies_and_interests: Deshmukh enjoys listening to devotional bhajans and Marathi folk songs, often humming tunes while cooking. He is an avid cricket fan, following domestic tournaments and the Indian team's matches on the radio. In his spare time, he cultivates a small kitchen garden, growing tomatoes, chilies and okra, and experiments with new Maharashtrian recipes. He likes reading simple Marathi novels and short stories, especially those that reflect rural life. Community festivals attract his participation, where he helps with decorations and traditional dances. Occasionally, he attends basic computer classes at the local community centre to learn simple spreadsheet skills for household budgeting. He also likes taking leisurely walks in the fields surrounding his village, appreciating nature and reflecting on daily tasks.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to devotional bhajans and Marathi folk songs', 'Following cricket matches and tournaments', 'Cultivating a kitchen garden with vegetables', 'Experimenting with new Maharashtrian recipes', 'Reading Marathi novels and short stories about rural life', 'Participating in community festivals (decorations, traditional dances)', 'Attending basic computer classes for budgeting', 'Taking leisurely walks in village fields']
career_goals_and_ambitions: At 51, Deshmukh aims to transition from sole household work to a modest family‑run domestic‑service venture that can generate steady income for his retirement and provide employment opportunities for younger relatives. He intends to formalise his expertise by offering training workshops on practical housekeeping, cooking, and basic home‑maintenance for aspiring helpers in his village. Parallelly, he dreams of supporting his children's aspirations—ensuring his son can pursue higher education in a technical field and his daughter can start a small tailoring business. He also wishes to invest in a small piece of land for vegetable cultivation, using it both as an extra source of income and as a means to maintain his connection to the agrarian lifestyle he cherishes.
persona_clinical_summary: A 51-year-old male from Kolhapur, Maharashtra, currently married, with middle education, working as a domestic housekeeper. He is a Marathi-speaking rural resident in the Kolhapur district, zone, with a history of community leadership. His lifestyle includes listening to cricket on the radio, practicing devotional bhajans, and managing household finances. He runs a modest domestic-service training venture and follows the Maharashtra Ranji team.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

मुख्य तक्रार:
रुग्णाला तीन दिवसांपासून तीव्र ताप, थंडी वाजणे आणि अंगदुखी होती. त्याने पिवळ्या कफसह सतत खोकला आणि सौम्य धाप लागल्याचेही सांगितले.
वैद्यकीय निष्कर्ष:
तपासणी दरम्यान, रुग्णाचे तापमान 102.4°F होते. त्याचा हृदयगती दर मिनिटाला 108 ठोके होता, श्वसन दर मिनिटाला 22 श्वास होता आणि रक्तदाब 130/85 mmHg होता. सामान्य हवेत ऑक्सिजन सॅच्युरेशन 94% होते. छातीच्या तपासणीत उजव्या खालच्या फुफ्फुसाच्या भागात जाड क्रॅकल्स (खडखडाट) आढळले. रक्त तपासणीत ल्युकोसाइटोसिससह न्यूट्रोफिलिया दिसून आला. छातीच्या क्ष-किरण (X-ray) तपासणीत उजव्या खालच्या लोबमध्ये कन्सोलिडेशन (एकत्रीकरण) दिसून आले.
निदान:
कम्युनिटी-अक्वायर्ड न्यूमोनियासह सेप्सिस.
उपचार योजना:
रुग्णाला शिरेवाटे (intravenous) प्रतिजैविके, तापशामक औषधे आणि द्रव पुनरुज्जीवन (fluid resuscitation) सुरू करण्यात आले. देखरेख आणि पुढील व्यवस्थापनासाठी त्याला जनरल वॉर्डमध्ये दाखल करण्यात आले.
डिस्चार्ज सारांश:
पाच दिवसांच्या उपचारानंतर, रुग्णाच्या प्रकृतीत लक्षणीय सुधारणा झाली. त्याचा ताप उतरला आणि श्वसनाच्या लक्षणांमध्ये सुधारणा झाली. त्याला २० ऑक्टोबर २०२४ रोजी तोंडावाटे प्रतिजैविके लिहून देण्यात आली आणि त्याच्या प्राथमिक उपचार डॉक्टरांकडे पाठवण्याचा सल्ला देण्यात आला.
टीपीए प्राधिकरण:
पॉलिसी अंतर्गत विमा रकमेपर्यंत कॅशलेस उपचारांसाठी अधिकृत.

Admission [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-1025]] on 15 October 2024.
[[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sunil Deshmukh]]
Chief Complaint:
Patient presented with acute onset of high-grade fever, chills, and generalized body ache for three days. He also reported a persistent cough with yellow sputum and mild shortness of breath.
Clinical Findings:
On examination, the patient was febrile with a temperature of 102.4°F. His heart rate was 108 beats per minute, respiratory rate was 22 breaths per minute, and blood pressure was 130/85 mmHg. Oxygen saturation was 94% on room air. Chest auscultation revealed coarse crackles in the right lower lung field. Blood investigations showed leukocytosis with neutrophilia. A chest X-ray demonstrated consolidation in the right lower lobe.
Diagnosis:
Community-acquired pneumonia with sepsis.
Treatment Plan:
The patient was started on intravenous antibiotics, antipyretics, and fluid resuscitation. He was admitted to the general ward for monitoring and further management.
Discharge Summary:
The patient showed significant clinical improvement after five days of treatment. Fever subsided, and his respiratory symptoms resolved. He was discharged on 20 October 2024 with a prescription for oral antibiotics and advised to follow up with his primary physician.
TPA Authorization:
Authorized for cashless treatment up to the sum insured under the policy.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) for clinical domain Surgical.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Nepali (code=ne, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=West Bengal, district=Darjiling.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Nepali.

PLACEMENT (OPD header): First line MUST include BOTH [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] (facility name) AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] (facility code) — never substitute one for the other.
Put [[EMPLOYEE_ID|…]] on the registrar/clerk line and [[RELATIVE_NAME|…]] on the attendant/relative line. Include [[DOB|YYYY-MM-DD]] and [[OCCUPATION|…]] with demographics.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 54
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Stock Clerks, Other
- education_level: Graduate & above
- zone: Urban
- state: West Bengal
- district: Darjiling
- first_language: Nepali
- clinical_persona_summary: A 54-year-old male stock clerk, currently married, with a graduate education working in an urban West Bengal district. Nepali is his first language, and he resides in Darjiling. His lifestyle includes a habit of cash savings and occasional health neglect.

Document format guide for OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (adapt language/script; keep tags):
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital X]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-X-01]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] | DOB [[DOB|1992-03-14]] | Age: [[AGE|34]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. ...]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|...]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-4412]] | District: [[DISTRICT|...]]
Chief complaint: <short clinical line matching assigned domain>

MANDATORY inline entities (13 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- OCCUPATION — Occupation e.g. [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- EMPLOYEE_ID — Employee ID e.g. [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

परीक्षण: दाहिने इन्गुइनल क्षेत्रमा लगभग 5x4 सेन्टिमिटरको, थिच्दा खुल्ने (reducible), दुख्ने गाँठो देखिन्छ, जुन नधड्किने (non-pulsatile) छ र छालामा कुनै परिवर्तन छैन। खोकी लगाउँदा कुनै धक्का महसुस भएन। आन्द्राको आवाज सबै चतुर्थांशमा उपस्थित छ।
निदान: दाहिने इन्गुइनल हर्निया, थिच्दा खुल्ने।
योजना: 1. हर्निया र शल्यक्रियाको आवश्यकताका बारेमा बिरामीलाई परामर्श दिने। 2. ऐच्छिक दाहिने इन्गुइनल हर्नियोराफीको तालिका बनाउने। 3. दुखाइ कम गर्ने औषधि (analgesics) सिफारिस गर्ने र भारी उठाउनबाट बच्न सल्लाह दिने। 4. एक हप्तामा शल्यक्रिया ओपिडीमा फलो-अप गर्ने।

persona: Ankur Gain, a 54-year-old inventory veteran with a competitive edge, they blend meticulous stock-keeping with a love for Himalayan art, folk music, and trekking, while habitually juggling cash savings and occasional health neglect.
cultural_background: Ankur was born and raised in the bustling hill town of Darjeeling, West Bengal, where the Nepali‑speaking Gorkha community forms a core part of the social fabric. Growing up amidst tea gardens, colonial‑era architecture and a vibrant mix of Nepali, Indian and Tibetan influences, he inherited the customs of a Hindu Nepali family—celebrating festivals like Dashain and Tihar, partaking in local folk music and dance, and being familiar with the tea‑garden lifestyle. The urban setting, with its constant flow of tourists and diverse cultural events, has also exposed him to varied artistic expressions and modern ideas, feeding his curiosity for art and new experiences. His outgoing nature makes him a familiar face at community gatherings, while his competitive streak drives him to excel in his work.
linguistic_background: Ankur's mother tongue is Nepali; he is completely literate in Nepali, able to read, write and speak fluently. His formal education, a graduate degree in a non‑technical field, required proficiency in English, so he can understand and use English for reading academic material and workplace communication. Living and working in Darjeeling has given him conversational ability in Hindi, primarily for interacting with customers and colleagues, though he does not consider himself fully proficient. He has little exposure to Bengali despite residing in West Bengal, and he does not have any additional foreign language skills.
skills_and_expertise: Ankur has over three decades of experience as a stock clerk, giving him strong expertise in inventory control, stock auditing, and supply‑chain basics. He is adept at using manual ledgers as well as computer‑based inventory software, performs accurate data entry, and maintains meticulous records. His role requires effective customer interaction, product knowledge, and basic accounting for stock reconciliation. He possesses good organizational abilities, time‑management, and team coordination skills, and he can handle high‑pressure situations in busy retail environments. His curiosity about art also equips him with a basic understanding of local handicrafts and cultural products, which helps in presenting items attractively.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Inventory Management', 'Stock Auditing', 'Data Entry', 'Customer Service', 'Basic Accounting', 'MS Office', 'Team Coordination', 'Supply Chain Basics', 'Product Knowledge', 'Art and Handicraft Presentation']
hobbies_and_interests: Ankur enjoys participating in group activities such as local cultural festivals, community gatherings and trekking trips in the Himalayas. He has a keen interest in Nepali folk music and dance, often attending live performances. His love for art leads him to visit galleries, collect small paintings and handicrafts, and experiment with sketching scenic tea‑garden landscapes. career_goals_and_ambitions: Ankur aspires to move into a supervisory or managerial role within inventory and supply‑chain operations, aiming to oversee multiple stockrooms across the Darjeeling region. He also wishes to mentor younger staff, helping them develop work discipline and appreciation for cultural heritage.
